Hi @mehdideveloper ,
glad to hear you want to add support for new SDRs! Here is a short guide to get you started:
1. add your new SDR name ``` src/urh/dev/BackendHandler.py ``` line 77 e.g. bladeRF
2. Next you have two options
  2.1 Create a __gnuradio backend__. You can look at the exisiting scripts in ``` src/urh/dev/gr/scripts ``` to get started. Make sure your script is named <lowercasename_of_your_sdr_from 1>_{send, recv} e.g. ``` bladrf_send.py ``` or ``` bladerf_recv.py ```
  2.2 Create a __native backend__ using cython. You can see an example for the hackrf in ``` src/urh/dev/native/lib/ ```. Look into ``` urh/dev/native/HackRF.py ``` to see how to use your wrapper with urh. Essentially you need to inherit from ``` urh/dev/native/Device.py ```.

It is preferred to create a native backend, if possible. However, creating a gnuradio backend requires less effort.

Let me know, if you need further assistance.

I'm a bit rusty on gevent, but I'm guessing that the `print` statement does some I/O that is lost unless you use gevent's monkey patching to gevent-enable print.

The following example works:

``` python
import gevent.monkey
gevent.monkey.patch_all()

import pykka
import pykka.gevent


class GeventPrintActor(pykka.gevent.GeventActor):
    def print_text(self, message):
        print message
        self.stop()


class ThreadingPrintActor(pykka.ThreadingActor):
    def print_text(self, message):
        print message
        self.stop()


GeventPrintActor.start().proxy().print_text('GeventPrintActor')
ThreadingPrintActor.start().proxy().print_text('ThreadingPrintActor')
```

If you don't do any I/O, `GeventActor` works without monkey patching anything:

``` python
import pykka
import pykka.gevent


class GeventPrintActor(pykka.gevent.GeventActor):
    def get_text(self):
        return 'GeventText'


class ThreadingPrintActor(pykka.ThreadingActor):
    def get_text(self):
        return 'ThreadingText'


gevent_proxy = GeventPrintActor.start().proxy()
threading_proxy = ThreadingPrintActor.start().proxy()

gevent_future = gevent_proxy.get_text()
threading_future = threading_proxy.get_text()

print gevent_future.get()
print threading_future.get()

gevent_proxy.stop()
threading_proxy.stop()
```

Or, as a modification of 3rd way, we can catch ImportError and re-throw it with altered message to make it obvious that problem is config-related:
```python
try:
    module = import_module(module)
    config.update...
except ImportError as exc:
    raise ImportError('Failed to load config module %s' % module) from exc
```
In such way, the user will see both original ImportError and a notification that it is config-related.

Could you add docstrings similar to other functions? The docstrings format in pysptk is under numpydoc style, I believe. In particular, I would like to document what `pitch` really means, since the SPTK `pitch` command outputs three kinds of pitch (see [/bin/pitch/pitch.c#L71-L74](https://github.com/r9y9/SPTK/blob/master/bin/pitch/pitch.c#L71-L74)) and I guess `pysptk.excite` assumes output format 0. It is actually different from that of pysas's excite assumes.

As for the docs, docstrings are automatically retrieved by sphinx and doc is generated if `autosummary` directive is found in the module `__doc__`. See example for [sptk.pyx#L74-L79](https://github.com/jfsantos/pysptk/blob/master/pysptk/sptk.pyx#L74-L79). Thanks!

Just thinking out loud about this N threads and N workers thing.    I wonder if you could make something like that work more sanely if you used a dedicated task to serve as an intermediary.  For example::

    thr_q = threading.Queue()
    cur_q = curio.Queue()

    async def bridge(thr_q, cur_q):
        while True:
             curio.abide(thr_q.put(await cur_q.get()))

Now, if you had a thousand curio tasks running and they wanted to communicate with the thread, they'd just put their items onto `cur_q`.   The `bridge()` task would take them and hand it over to the thread.   Only one thread would be needed.

Not suggesting that this would be the "solution" to efficient Curio-Thread communication ;-).  It just seemed like an interesting approach that would greatly reduce the number of background threads needed to have N worker tasks put things on a thread-queue using existing machinery.

There should be one pass per library, so everything is as expected. I don't see anything strange in the output. I'm however not surprised that there is not much contiguity increase in the assembly after scaffolding if you are only using the PE library that was used in the original contig assembly. A scaffolder usually leverages mate pair reads that can span repeats etc to improve the contiguity of the assembly. In this case I'm assuming that most/all repetitive structures that was not solved by SPAdes is neither solved by BESST --- with this PE library. Another reason that the contigs are very fragmented could be high heterozygosity level of the genome you are trying to assemble, but this would pose an issue for both BESST and SPAdes.  In summary, I wouldn't hope for big gains over the original contig assembly if using only this library to scaffold.

from pyspider.result import ResultWorker
from types import MethodType

class MyMongoResultWorker(ResultWorker):
    def on_result(self, task, result):
        if not result:
            return
        if 'taskid' in task and 'project' in task and 'url' in task:
            logger.info('result %s:%s %s -> %.30r' % (
                task['project'], task['taskid'], task['url'], result))
            self._dynamic_result_db()
            return self.resultdb.save(
                project=task['project'],
                taskid=task['taskid'],
                url=task['url'],
                result=result
            )
        else:
            logger.warning('result UNKNOW -> %.30r' % result)
            return


    def _dynamic_result_db(self):
        '''dynamic override the _stringify method of mongo resultdb'''
        def _stringify(self, data):
            return data

        self.resultdb._stringify = MethodType(_stringify, self.resultdb)
```

The python bindings will use SSE4 by default, but not AVX.

There is a lot of variation in how boost.python is installed.  It's a frequent user complaint that they get errors from mixing boost.python versions with the wrong version of python, or it's installed in some weird place.  I don't really think this is a CMake problem.  How else would we compile these modules?  You need some reliable way to find the version of boost.python and python libs that match the version of python the user is going to be running.  If that could be solved then the setup.py could pass that information to cmake and it should be fine.

For a point of comparison, I made [MITIE](https://github.com/mit-nlp/MITIE) and it uses CMake to build python extensions and no one ever has any problems with it.  But it doesn't use boost.python.  Instead I wrote a C API which uses ctypes from python.  So there is no dependency on python libs or boost or anything when compiling the shared object.  However, writing a C API and binding it through ctypes is a huge pain in the ass.  It's only reasonable to do with a small API. 

Is there some way to find boost.python and python libs in setup.py and verrify that they are compiled together correctly?  I think that's the heart of the issue.

Yes, you need to have CUDA and cudNN installed to use any CUDA related accelerations. Just running `python setup.py install` is fine.  Despite all the nonsense on the internet, you never need to give `--yes DLIB_USE_CUDA`.  The command `--yes DLIB_USE_CUDA` literally does nothing and has always done nothing.  You could say `--no DLIB_USE_CUDA` and it would disable CUDA though.

If are going to use the python module on a CPU with AVX instructions then you can say `--yes USE_AVX_INSTRUCTIONS` and some things will be faster.  The default has been to not use AVX unless the users asks.  However, the code currently in git now checks if the host computer supports AVX and automatically enables it for python modules.  So anyone using the latest dlib doesn't have to say `--yes USE_AVX_INSTRUCTIONS` and it is literally a noop now.  However, `--no USE_AVX_INSTRUCTIONS` can disable them just like `--no` works for CUDA.

After doing some calculations, I get an idea of what might be the issue here: The snmpd sends a Counter32-Value of length 3 (0x8ba43c). Like snmpwalk, PySNMP prefixes this to 0xff8ba43c. The issue with PySNMP now is, that it interprets this as a negative number, -7625668, not as a positive number which would be 4287341628 (and therefore perfectly fit in the ValueRangeConstraint).
Sadly, I do not know the code of PySNMP good enough to provide a fix, but maybe this is a help aswell.

If the system keyring is GNOME Keyring, you can use [SecretStorage](https://pypi.python.org/pypi/SecretStorage) module (which is usually the default Python-Keyring backend on GNOME).

For example:

    :::python3
    import secretstorage
    bus = secretstorage.dbus_init()
    collection = secretstorage.get_default_collection()
    for item in collection.get_all_items():
        print(item.get_label())
        print(item.get_attributes())

Instead of using `get_all_items()` you can also specify a filter using `search_items()` method. See [the documentation](http://pythonhosted.org/SecretStorage/) for details.

Also, you can use GNOME’s libgnome-keyring (the legacy library) and libsecret (the new library) in Python via GObject introspection.

If you'd like to generate a token with a longer expiration, simply set the `exp` claim in your token.
For instance:
```
import jwt
import datetime
jwt.encode({'exp': datetime.datetime.now() + datetime.timedelta(days=1)}, key='somesecret')
```

Yes, empty key is handled as a keyed hash function with zero-length key, which is different from not having a key, because in this case the first data block is set to the "key" padded with zeros (i.e all zeros). The BLAKE2 spec says that key length in the parameter block should be set to 0 if there's no key, but doesn't say what to do if the key present but is zero-length. In both pyblake2 and blake2 Go packages I interpreted the latter case to still set key length 0 zero and continue processing as if we have a key, which I think is a correct choice.

So, it's up to the package users to select the proper hash function they need: a keyed or unkeyed one. I should also note that your examples should read:

> `pyblake2.blake2b('')` does not equal `pyblake2.blake2b('', key='')` 
> (or `b''` if using Python 3)

Thus, there should be no confusion for programmers on which function is used.

Hi,

I just see that I didn't seem to have implemented something like that.


There are some easy ways to do that using pandas base functionality. E.g., to decrease the residue numbers by 1 you can simply do

    ppdb.df['ATOM']['residue_number'] -= 1

However, if the residue numbers are not in order, or if there are gaps, like (1, 2, 10, 20), which you want to rename to (1, 2, 3, 4, 5), you would have to do it differently. E.g.,

you could first get all the unique residue numbers in the order they appear:

```python
from collections import OrderedDict

ordered_unique_elements = \
    list(OrderedDict.fromkeys(ppdb.df['ATOM']['residue_number']))
```

and then map from the old residue numbers to the new, contiguous residue numbers:

```python
mapping_dict = {ordered_unique_elements[i]: i+1 
                for i in range(0, len(ordered_unique_elements))}

ppdb.df['ATOM']['residue_number'] = \
    ppdb.df['ATOM']['residue_number'].map(mapping_dict)
```

I could actually add that as a method to BioPandas, or maybe just explain it in documentation. What do you think?

@DudleyFox I just published version 1.2.0, which now supports little-endian nbt data. I added a `byteorder` argument to most functions and methods. It defaults to `'big'` for backward compatibility but you can set it to `'little'` to use the little-endian format.

```python
parsed_compound = Compound.parse(some_file, byteorder='little')
```

I also added an example in the [examples](https://github.com/vberlier/nbtlib/tree/master/examples) folder that showcases how you could use `nbtlib` to read and edit level.dat files from Bedrock-based Minecraft editions. The `level_dat_bedrock.py` example defines a `BedrockLevelFile` class that takes care of parsing the header and the content of the file using the little-endian format.

```python
# This is how you would edit a level.dat file

with BedrockLevelFile.load('level.dat') as level_data:
    level_data['LevelName'] = 'Something'
```

For the sake of convenience, I attached a schema that matches the structure of the level.dat file to the `BedrockLevelFile` class, but that's completely optional.

BlueZ is just about impossible to get working as a peripheral. I think something was deprecated that causes "register GATT service with handle" when the master tries to read services. It sure would be nice to have BluePy and less BlueZ dependent alternative for the new Raspberry Pi 3 which has BT and LE out of the box!

It looks like it's a limitation of Bluetooth LE itself. Connecting to a peripheral is done with the **LE_Create_Connection** command; the Bluetooth 4.0 spec says (vol 2, sec 7.8.12, page 826)

> The Host shall not issue this command when another LE_Create_Connection is pending in the
> Controller; if this does occur the Controller shall return the Command Disallowed error code shall 
> be used.

Sadly, then, it's built in to the HCI command specification that only one connection can be pending at a a time.

```python
while 1:
    time.sleep(10)

Without them, the program's main thread will end right away, and the `MessageLoop`, which is run as a second thread, will evaporate. You need to keep the program alive for `MessageLoop` to keep working.

I think you did exactly the right thing by just setting last_recv to None. The code right afterward can check to see if a failure should be raised, based on `self._check_data_received_each_request`.

Here are some suggestions, but they aren't necessarily a prerequisite for accepting the PR.
 1. One of my semi long-term refactoring goals is to move all `socket` code out of `sessions.py`. The ideal place to catch this exception would be in `socket_connection.py`.
 2. You can define a new exception in `boofuzz/sex.py`. You might call it something like `BoofuzzConnectionReset`. Then, in `socket_connection.py` `recv()`, you'd catch the socket error and raise your custom exception.
3. Finally, catch the custom exception in sessions.py.

This might seem like a goofy run-around, but the major upshot is that it keeps `sessions.py` socket-agnostic. Then, any new connection layers that people write will be able to hook into all the new functionality just by raising your custom exception when the connection is reset.

Another simpler option would be to just catch the exception in `socket_connection.py` and return no data. Actually, I think that might be all that's necessary, unless `sessions.py` ever needs to do anything special in that case.

The exception is originating from Channel's __del__ method, i.e.

```
# aio-pika/channel.py
# line 335
def __del__(self):
    with suppress(Exception):
        self.loop.create_task(self.close())
```

What happens is if a channel has been explicitly closed with a call to `close()`, the exception will be raised whenever the above `__del__` method is invoked (i.e. the object is garbage collected) - the future returned by `self.loop.create_task(self.close())` is left hanging, and we get a cryptic error message from asyncio that nothing has handled the exception.

It is generally considered bad practice to utilize __del__ for resource cleanup (some discussion around that topic can be found here: https://stackoverflow.com/questions/6104535/i-dont-understand-this-python-del-behaviour) - instead, to achieve implicit channel cleanup, I'm of the opinion channels should be available using an asynchronous context manager, i.e.

```
async with connection.channel() as channel:
    # Do something with the channel
# Channel object has been discarded, and explicitly closed
```

If a channel object has been retrieved without using a context manager, then it is the developers responsibility to explicitly close it when it is no longer needed.

I did a quick grep search to see where seek is used in pydicom, and unfortunately it is used in quite a few places, especially in reading.  I was thinking of the memory buffer idea; sometimes the seek is used to rewind a small number of bytes. But I checked a few other places and sometimes the rewind (or reading forward) is potentially quite large.  It would be quite difficult to use a buffer for reading.

I found an issue on the python tracker: http://bugs.python.org/issue22908, which offers a diff to add seek ability to ZipExtFile, but it does so by rewinding to the beginning and reading forward to the new position on every seek, so it likely degrades performance significantly unless files are small enough to stay in cache.  Maybe a smarter version of it could store state at various points in the file to reduce the amount of re-reading.

Here is a potential workaround that you can use, although it still requires editing Python code files.

- Use `django.utils.translation.ugettext_noop` to mark strings as translatable without actually translating them (see <https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/1.8/topics/i18n/translation/#marking-strings-as-no-op> for more information).
- Or put all of your custom strings in a file that never gets used anywhere; `makemessages` will still find and parse it.
- Or all of the above.

For example:

1. Create a file named `strings.py` somewhere in your Django project:
  ```
  > $EDITOR strings.py
  ```

2. Add custom strings wrapped with `ugettext_noop`:
  ```
  from django.utils.translation import ugettext_noop as _

  _('Custom String 1')
  _('Custom String 2')
  ```

3. Create `*.po` files:
  ```
  > python manage.py makemessages --all
  ```

4. Custom strings are included in locale files:

See #120 for a discussion of `executemany` performance. TL;DR: It is often not much faster than performing individual inserts in a loop. Note that this is true not only for pyodbc but for other Python ODBC layers as well.

A commonly-suggested workaround is to use a bulk copy utility such as the `bcp` utility for Microsoft SQL Server, but Microsoft Access does not have such a stand-alone utility. The MS Access equivalent is to use VBA code to import data from text files via VBA's `DoCmd.TransferText` method. 

If you really need to accomplish your task via ODBC from within Python, then one alternative that *might* offer a modest performance gain would be to 

- create a temporary table with no indexes, possibly in a [separate database file](https://github.com/gordthompson/msaccessdb),
- insert the data into the temporary table while autocommit=False, and then
- `INSERT INTO real_table (...) SELECT ... FROM temp_table`

This is a windows socket library problem, so I'm poking around in the dark here.  According to [this question](http://stackoverflow.com/questions/4657347/socket-error-ip-address-not-valid-in-its-context-python):

> That's an error Windows gives when you're trying to bind to an address on the local machine that's not assigned to any of the adapters on the machine. If ipconfig doesn't show it, you can't bind to it.

So you can't bind to the LAN broadcast address, but the question is, can you receive packets sent to this address?  My guess is that Windows expects you to want to receive broadcast packets, and it may recognize both LAN broadcast and generic 255.255.255.255 broadcast packets.  This is not the same for datagram packets in Linux.  You'll have to test that theory on a LAN with two Windows machines.

The `broadcastPort` is only used to listen for broadcast messages, it's not used for sending them.  The destination for a broadcast is calculated from the address, so that part works independently of having this special socket.

Someplace I've got some sample code kicking around that will make this easy to test, I'll create a `issue-59` branch and add it.

You can pull data, but you would need to register your hooks to collect this data. For example:
```python
def collect(value):
    pass  # logic to collect value. The method will be called every time the value of the char changes

class TemperatureSensor(Accessory):

    category = CATEGORY_SENSOR

    def __init__(self, *args, **kwargs):
        super().__init__(*args, **kwargs)

        serv_temp = self.add_preload_service('TemperatureSensor')
        # Add a callback to the temperature characteristic
        self.char_temp = serv_temp.configure_char('CurrentTemperature', setter_callback=collect)

    @Accessory.run_at_interval(3)
    def run(self):
        self.char_temp.set_value(random.randint(18, 26))
```

For listing accessories (I assume you will be using a Bridge, otherwise it's just one accessory) you can just do `bridge.accessories`.

For the attributes - what exactly do you need? An accessory is pretty much a container for services, which in turn are containers for characteristics.

Eek. I'd not thought about this use case at all.

So I know why this doesn't work, and for the moment it's not going to. I'll have a think about how to support this as an explicit use case, probably by special casing functions which return generators, but I don't currently have any great ideas.

In order to understand what's happening:

When you pass a function to the @given decorator, Hypothesis is basically returning a new function which mimics the signature of the old, but what actually happens when called is that it is calling your function many times with a different arguments and seeing if any of them cause it to error. It doesn't pay any attention to the return value because it assume it's a "normal" unit test which just either returns None or errors, so it doesn't notice that you've given it this generator and expected it to do something about that, and as a result it just goes "Well I guess that worked then!" and tries the next value immediately.

Hi Jeff,

In Excel, and in XlsxWriter a cell format overrides a row format overrides a column format.

Pandas is already applying a format to the header to the row format has no affect.

The usual workaround is the one suggested by Shijo on StackOverflow: to unset `pd.core.format.header_style` or `pd.core.format.header_style`, depending on your version of Pandas.

Regards,

John.

There isn't some flag you can just set or easy command you can send to do this, so you'll have to be a little creative. For example, if I wanted to use a script to play a preroll before watching show X, I'd have that script first have a play action for the preroll I wanted to play, and then a second action to play show X. There are numerous ways you can handle knowing when the preroll is done to have it then play the desired show. You could make use of the duration information available in the objects you play to sleep a certain amount of time between the plays. Other options include using the EventListener to react based on play/stop, or react to webhooks, provided you make something to listen for them. There are bound to be others. Using a sleep based on duration is probably the easiest to start with IMO, but someone else might feel different on that subject.

> Changes the return type of mutator.getAxisNames() from a list to a set

The docstring of `getAxisNames` says it returns "a list of axis names from all deltas". Even though MutatorMath itself uses this method only in `Location.expand` method which simply iterates over it, it's not safe to change its type to a `dict_keys` object. This is similar to a set in the sense that it's not sorted, but it's actually a "view" into the original dictionary (e.g. its contents are not independent from it).
Other libraries or scripts may depend on the fact that the getAxisNames method returns a list object, so I think it's safer to keep it as such and just fix up the doctest by using `sorted()` instead of the current `list()` whose ordering is not guaranteed.

The headers are always supposed to be there, but they won't. Amazon is not the only client that sends request to your skill, there are various bots for example. Also, while testing your skill, Amazon will use requests without the Signature header. If you don't globally catch KeyErrors (which feels a bit wrong) you end up returning a 500 status code.

I don't think Flask Ask should throw a KeyError. It should simply throw the same ValidationError it throws on an invalid certificate, that would be the simplest and cleanest approach.

I totally agree about hiding the traceback. We should change `src/wormhole/cli/cli.py send()` to catch `TransferError` and only display the stringified exception object.

For reference, the traceback (and the TransferError exception object itself) are entirely local: the only part that comes from the remote system is the "file already exists" string that is passed as an argument into the TransferError constructor. So the remote system is in control of how much information it reveals.

I guess I'm on the fence about whether the receiver should reveal this or not. I think it's handy, sometimes. If you search for `RespondError` in `src/wormhole/cli/cmd_receive.py`, that gives a list of all the strings that the recipient might send back in their error message:

* `unknown offer type`: probably only if the sender has a newer version, and they're sending something other than a file or directory.. this might happen some day if we handle #58 by offering a different protocol for streaming transfers
* `unknown mode`: we send a directory, and compress it with an unrecognized "zip" mode (something other than "DEFLATE").. unlikely to ever happen
* `FILENAME already exists`
* `transfer rejected` (when the receiving user says "no" to the "accept file?" prompt)

The first two are internal errors that indicate a version mismatch, and the last is clearly user-provoked.

I guess I'll agree that file-already-exists could be reported as "transfer rejected" without losing too much.. the sender's behavior is the same (re-send the file, give the recipient a new code). And really, maybe we should change the receiver code to give the recipient a chance to pick an alternative file name if the target is already present (also see #73), in which case the recipient's choice to *not* allow an alternative name would be morally equivalent to rejecting the transfer, making it more correct to report the same `transfer rejected` code for both cases.

Ok, so the action items are:

* catch `TransferError`, show it's `str()` and `sys.exit(1)` instead of allowing the exception to rise. (note: the function uses a Deferred, so it needs `d.addErrback()` and `f.trap(TransferError)` instead of the usual `try/except`)
* replace `RespondError("%s already exists")` with `RespondError("transfer rejected")` in `cmd_receive.py`

Metadata is also only meaningful in `ia upload` on item creation (i.e. on the first upload). After that it is ignored, and `ia metadata` must be used (this has a lot to do with the underlying APIs. In the future, it might make sense to work around this in the `ia` client and update the metadata in every `ia upload` request. That's not currently the case, though). So I think what you're seeing here is the initial connection that get's made to the host over SMB so that it can pull the host information (OS, architecture, domain, etc...). The reasoning behind this is because we need the domain name the machine is joined to to authenticate the DCOM connection to execute a command via WMI. That's why CME requires SMB to be enabled to execute WMI commands (or really anything that goes over DCOM). Now, in theory we could execute a WMI command without first connecting to the target via SMB and just specify all the information on the command line, but this would require a pretty extensive re-write since this is just how CME works right now. Furthermore, I have yet to see an environment that blocks SMB but leaves the RPC port open, and I'm pretty sure this isn't a common scenario so I don't think it would be worth the effort to accomadate this edge-case (but i'm definitely open to being corrected).

I had a look into this and got it working using these properties:

```python
from exchangelib import Message, ExtendedProperty

class VoteRequest(ExtendedProperty):
    property_set_id = '00062008-0000-0000-C000-000000000046'
    property_id = 0x00008520
    property_type = 'Binary'

class VoteResponse(ExtendedProperty):
    property_set_id = '00062008-0000-0000-C000-000000000046'
    property_id = 0x00008524
    property_type = 'String'

Message.register('vote_request', VoteRequest)
Message.register('vote_response', VoteResponse)
```

The vote request value is a binary encoded in a `VerbStream`. Here's a link with a script showing how to decode the stream: http://gsexdev.blogspot.dk/2015/01/ A helper method in exchangelib to do the encoding would be very helpful.

See #230 regarding the magic values in the props above.

It may not be clear from the docs, but the input to a unicodecsv reader is expected to be bytes (str in python2), not unicode, so you should be using BytesIO rather than StringIO.

Testing with BytesIO rather than StringIO, I do see the "new-line character seen in unquoted field" error.  I think this is a bug in the underlying csv module - https://docs.python.org/2/library/csv.html#csv.Dialect.lineterminator "The reader is hard-coded to recognise either '\r' or '\n' as end-of-line, and ignores lineterminator. This behavior may change in the future."

Using \r (as with your original data), I can reproduce this with a normal file, not io.BytesIO or io.StringIO -- and of course unicodecsv does generally work with files.

When I change your data to use \n rather than \r, then the code works:

````
from io import StringIO, BytesIO
from unicodecsv import DictReader, Dialect, QUOTE_MINIMAL

data = (
    'first_name,last_name,email\n'
    'Elmer,Fudd,elmer@looneytunes.com\n'
    'Jo\xc3\xa3o Ant\xc3\xb4nio,Ara\xc3\xbajo,joaoantonio@araujo.com\n'
)

unicode_data = StringIO(unicode(data, 'utf-8-sig'), newline=None)
str_data = BytesIO(data)

class CustomDialect(Dialect):
    delimiter = ','
    doublequote = True
    escapechar = '\\'
    lineterminator = '\r'
    quotechar = '"'
    quoting = QUOTE_MINIMAL
    skipinitialspace = True

rows = DictReader(str_data, dialect=CustomDialect)
````

Unfortunately I don't see a way to fix this from within unicodecsv.

Face distance isn't exactly the same thing as percentage match, but it serves the same purpose and is the closest thing you can do.

If no face is found in the image, the encodings array will be empty. So check the length of the array before you try to access the first element (i.e. element `[0]`) of the array.

For example:

```python
encodings = face_recognition.face_encodings(known_image)

    biden_encoding = encodings[0]
else:
   print("No faces found in the image!")
   quit()
``

Hi,

I wanted to explain this by updating the docs on this, but let's start here first. A transfer function maps voxel values in the range `[0, 1]` to rgba (rgb+alpha) colors. In ipvolume, a volumetric cube is first normalized to the `[0, 1]` range, unless datamin, datamax is provided, where clipping is applied when the values are outside this range. The default transfer function exists of 3 gaussian bumps, where the location of the bumps are provided in `level`, the sigmas/widths by `level_width`, and the height/opacity by `opacity`. The first bump causes will result in red values, the 2nd in green, and the 3rd in blue. Note that this is the default transfer function, it should be possible (but haven't tested that for a while) to define your own transfer function.

Would really like to have an example reproducing for instance [this](http://graphicsrunner.blogspot.nl/2009/01/volume-rendering-102-transfer-functions.html)

Does this help? I will keep this open until transfer functions are better documented.

#!/usr/bin/env python
# Copyright (C) 2013-2016  Vincent Pelletier <plr.vincent@gmail.com>

# License along with this library; if not, write to the Free Software
# Foundation, Inc., 51 Franklin Street, Fifth Floor, Boston, MA  02110-1301  USA
import usb1, threading

eventType = {   usb1.HOTPLUG_EVENT_DEVICE_ARRIVED: 'arrived',
                usb1.HOTPLUG_EVENT_DEVICE_LEFT: 'left', }

def outside_event_context(device):
    try:
        print "Device SerialNumber: %s (outside context)" % device.getSerialNumber()
    except Exception as e:
        if type(e) is usb1.USBErrorNoDevice:
            return 

        print "device.getSerialNumber failed with: %s (%s) (outside context)" % (e, type(e).__name__)        
        with usb1.USBContext() as context:
            device_ = context.getByVendorIDAndProductID(device.getVendorID(), device.getProductID())
            serialNumber_ = device_.getSerialNumber()
            print "Fetching device with new usbContext fix usb_busy error: %s (outside context)" % serialNumber_

def hotplug_callback(context, device, event):
    print "Device %s: %s - %s" % (eventType[event], device, device.getNumConfigurations())

    try:
        print "Device SerialNumber: %s" % device.getSerialNumber()
    except Exception as e:
        if type(e) is usb1.USBErrorNoDevice:
            return 

        print "device.getSerialNumber failed with: %s (%s)" % (e, type(e).__name__)        
        with usb1.USBContext() as context:
            device_ = context.getByVendorIDAndProductID(device.getVendorID(), device.getProductID())
            serialNumber_ = device_.getSerialNumber()
            print "Fetching device with new usbContext fix usb_busy error: %s" % serialNumber_

    threading.Timer(15, outside_event_context, [device]).start()
    pass

def main():
    with usb1.USBContext() as context:
        if not context.hasCapability(usb1.CAP_HAS_HOTPLUG):
            print 'Hotplug support is missing. Please update your libusb version.'
            return
        print 'Registering hotplug callback...'
        opaque = context.hotplugRegisterCallback(hotplug_callback)
        print 'Callback registered. Monitoring events, ^C to exit'
        try:
            while True:
                context.handleEvents()
        except (KeyboardInterrupt, SystemExit):
            print 'Exiting'

if __name__ == '__main__':
    main()
```

I suspected that hyperthreading would be why it would return more than the number of physical cores. However, hyperthreading (at least in a 2011 machine; some things apparently changed with 2015+ Intel processors) primarily benefits non-cpu-bound threads, as I understand it; it allows them to wait on I/O (usually) while being able to quickly be switched into execution when that other resource is available. In other words, whether your machine, for instance, should be doing 4 or 8 evaluation processes will depend on whether the fitness function is CPU-bound or not. I'll see if I can put something into the documentation about this. (I confess I hadn't thought about this re hyperthreading quite this explicitly before; I just know that anything above a load average of 2 radically slows down the machine.)

@Drekin `win-unicode-console` should do *nothing* unless the input/output is Windows console (`colorama` module is a good example) i.e., there is no reason to fail on Unix. The use-case is writing scripts that can print Unicode on multiple platforms without requiring users to run your scripts using `py -mrun print_unicode.py` on Windows. 

Also, `win-unicode-console` may provide useful behavior even on Unix (or if the output is redirected to a file/pipe on any OS) e.g., if the environment is misconfigured by accident to use ASCII locale (ssh, sudo) then `win-unicode-console` may allow to force utf-8 anyway (via **opt-in** parameter).  Thus Python script may print Unicode in whatever environment.

The database encoding is used for the client encoding by default.
http://www.postgresql.org/docs/9.5/static/runtime-config-client.html#GUC-CLIENT-ENCODING

So you can change it using `initidb_args`::
```
>>> from testing.postgresql import Postgresql
>>> import psycopg2
>>> pgsql = Postgresql(initdb_args='-E=EUC_JP --locale=ja_JP.eucJP -U postgres -A trust')
>>> c = psycopg2.connect(**pgsql.dsn())
>>> c.encoding
'EUCJP'
```

Yeah I agree, I work in infosec, and it is obvious that there is nothing 100% secure, it's all about risk assessment.

In this particular case, I think the small project I'm working in does not actually have any really sensitive data, and therefore I think the approach of using the `token_freshness` pattern + adding some extra context to the `access_token` + lowering the life time of `expiry_time` to something like a few hours instead of days will do it.

A few thoughts though that I found interesting from your last comment:

-  > If you are basing it off an ip address, the attacker might have access to the same computer or it might be a different user on the NAT. 
True, but I believe that if as an attacker you are on the same local network, or even worse on the same computer, there are many more nasty things you can do. So I still find valid the approach of adding context to the tokens as a reasonable way of increasing the security of the tokens while keeping everything stateless.

- On blacklists and token revocation. I don't think there is something wrong with this approach, and if you have an app that has sensitive data this is possibly a must in order to minimize damages in case of token compromise. However, I dislike it in the sense that it goes against the whole idea of tokens and statelessness, do you know what I mean?. If you are at the point where you need to add state to your architecture, why not using cookies then?

- I was not familiar with the concept of `fresh tokens`, is that something you came up with?. I think it is a neat idea and reasonable compromise, and combining it to the idea of `context` can be a good alternative to blacklisting and revoking (depending on the nature of your app of course).

- > and you can use the `@fresh_jwt_required` decorator to prevent these tokens from accessing the most sensitive parts of your application (changing passwords, [...]
Quick note on this, changing password functionality should ask you for your old password, so I wouldn't consider necessary to flag this functionality, but of course, you can use it for other sensitive stuff such as adding CC details, modifying your profile, etc.

All in all, and this is a personal opinion, I'd try to stay away from token revokation and anything that involves adding more load on the server, as I think this goes against the principle of JWT. If your app really needs this cause of security or other reasons, then maybe JWT is not the best option? Also, I believe combining the ideas of `fresh tokens` and `context` may give you enough control and reasonable security, but of course, this has to be assessed on a case by case basis.

Cheers!

I was also curious why wasn't `simplekv` included in requirements.txt?

It fails that way because it is totally vulnerable to a cross site forgery request (https://www.owasp.org/index.php/Cross-Site_Request_Forgery_(CSRF)). The solution is to implement the strategy outline in the paragraphs bellow the first code block here (http://flask-jwt-extended.readthedocs.io/en/latest/tokens_in_cookies.html) to perform a double submit token verification verification (https://www.owasp.org/index.php/Cross-Site_Request_Forgery_(CSRF)_Prevention_Cheat_Sheet#Double_Submit_Cookie). This insures that no CSRF attack is happening. 

The tl;dr version is you need to grab the CSRF token via javascript in your frontend and pass that in as an additional header to any state changing request that you make to your backend (POST, PUT, PATCH, DELET by default).

This extension is not vulnerable to the style of attacks described by these vulnerabilities, as we specify what algorithm to use to decode the token based on the `JWT_ALGORITHM` option, not on what the algorithm is defined in the JWT. This means our tokens only take up a little more space, but the performance hit there should be completely negligible.

I don't think it would be be too bad to change our JWTs to headless JWTs, it could be done with some `join` and `split` calls, and using the `JWT_ALGORITHM` to re-create the headers when decoding.

However, that may cause problems if these JWTs are being used in conjunction with other JWT providers/consumers, or if the JWT spec changed in the future and the underlying library we are using to make JWTs (pyjwt) updated their stuff to match. I would feel better about this if it was an option that we could pass to the underlying `jwt.encode()` and `jwt.decode()` functions, instead of tacked on to this extension.

It looks like there is a few differences between how this extension and flask-wtf is handing CSRF protection. My application is using a double submit cookie, whereas I think flask-wtf is using an encrypted token pattern. Either should work fine, you can read more about them here if you are so inclined:

https://www.owasp.org/index.php/Cross-Site_Request_Forgery_(CSRF)_Prevention_Cheat_Sheet

Either of these libraries should work to prevent csrf attacks, I can't think of a reason why you should use both. I would recommend disabling csrf protection on one of the libraries and just using the csrf protection on the other library.

My application is doing csrf protection on all the endpoints (GET included), compared to just POST/PUT/PATCH that flask-wtf is doing. Mine is also probably a bit harder to use if you are using are using a traditional website (not a single page application) compared to flask-jwt. These might influence your choice of what library you want to handle the CSRF protection.

Interesting. I guess that's something that should be addressed at the `TranslatableModelSerializer` level then, it should not create untranslated instances, but always create at least an instance in current or default language, even if its fields are empty.

I see where this happens. When `update()`-ing models, it always ensure a translation exists, creating it if it is missing. But for initial `create()`-ion, it relies on the default behavior, assuming there will always be at least one translated field or a language_code.
But, if using the form in non-_enforce_ mode, this may be incorrect.

I will look into it. In the meantime, I suggest you force a language on the serializer, by either of the following means:
  * Pass it a specific language to use when instanciating it. All models will be in that language.
  * Pass it `language=None` when instanciating it. This will force using detected browser language.
  * Have your javascript always specifiy a `language_code` field when creating a model.

Overriding `get_queryset` will work too, but will leave the instances untranslated, which will be a problem in the long run. By the way, if you need help adding default translations back to models that have none right now, just tell.

Fixed on master.
As a sidenote, not setting `fields` at all, or setting `fields = None` used to have exactly the same result. The `__all__` thing is a recent addition do Django. And what Django does with it is pretty straightforward: `if meta.fields == '__all__': meta.fields = None`.

Alright, so that depends on what you are trying to achieve.

- your form may display everything in one language. Either everything in English or everything in French.
    - what happens for objects that do not exist in current language then ? Are they shown in the other language, or are they hidden?
- your form may show both language at the same time. You probably need some custom javascript so it looks nice (like, creating tabs or something).

First option is pretty easy to achieve, but you must know which language you want to use. Either you ask the user which language he wants, or you choose based on his browser preferred language or you enforce a language based on business requirements (like "English is our main language, so everything must be English by default").

Second option allows editing both English and French on the same form, but it is more complex to code, and you risk confusing users who are not aware they should fill in both languages. That's usually more suited for some administration backend.

import numpy as np

import pwlf

data = pd.read_csv('ref_msd.16.bin', sep='\s+', engine='python', usecols=(0, 3), skiprows=1, names=('lag', 'msd_shift'))
lag = np.log10(data['lag'].as_matrix())

myPWLF = pwlf.PiecewiseLinFit(lag, msd_shift)

The PCIe-6536 does support hardware timing but only on ports 0-3.  Port 4 is PFI lines which can be used for either software timed static IO (which is what you are doing above) or for importing or exporting timing signals (i.e. sample clock, sample clock timebase).

See http://zone.ni.com/reference/en-XX/help/371893D-01/6536and6537help/frontpanel_6535_6/

If you need deterministic timing you should switch to a pin on ports 0-3.  You will not be able to achieve highly deterministic timing on Port 4 since system jitter will dominate.  You can make it more deterministic by raising the priority of the thread doing the software time IO, but it will still have pretty significant jitter and may not meet the requirements for your application.

The permission is mainly needed for updating configurations such as environment variables, memory size, and lambda timeouts. It is interesting that you are running into this when creating lambda functions. I would have expected it only happens for updating lambda functions. The tricky part about it is that in order to get this to work, there needs to be more granularity with IAM policies (i.e. deny `lambda:UpdateFunctionConfiguration` for Vpc configuration parameters and not just all parameters). It may be possible to add a flag to not update the function configuration or have the deployer be smarter about when it actually needs to make an `UpdateFunctionConfiguration` call. The only downside is that you would never be able to update properties such as environment variables, memory size, and lambda timeouts.

I think one option maybe worth exploring is to be a little more granular with the roles that you are using. So for those trying to use the console, they would have a role with `lambda:UpdateFunctionConfiguration` denied, and then for those using chalice, they could use a different role that would include permissions to `lambda:UpdateFunctionConfiguration`. Another to note is that Chalice does not support VPC configuration as of now. So deploying with chalice should not affect it. However, that may change once we do support VPC's (but it would be exposed through configuration nonetheless).

Let us know your thoughts on these options.

UnicodeEncodeError. ensure_unicode_string() tries to convert a string to unicode, and if that fails with a UnicodeDecodeError, it will *decode* the string with utf-8. In this situation, I believe there are cases where writing to the stream will try to convert it to a byte string, which may fail with a UnicodeEncodeError if it contains non-ascii characters. It may not be "wrong" in the sense that it will execute properly, but it looks like explicitly linking to a dynamic library without referencing any of its symbols prevents `ldd` from constructing the corresponding symbol table for the reference to that dynamic object (libc). The dynamic section looks like this:
```
$ readelf -d hello

Dynamic section at offset 0x1a8 contains 8 entries:
  Tag        Type                         Name/Value
 0x0000000000000001 (NEEDED)             Shared library: [libc.so.6]
 0x0000000000000004 (HASH)               0x400178
 0x0000000000000005 (STRTAB)             0x400188
 0x0000000000000006 (SYMTAB)             0x400188
 0x000000000000000a (STRSZ)              11 (bytes)
 0x000000000000000b (SYMENT)             24 (bytes)
 0x0000000000000015 (DEBUG)              0x0
 0x0000000000000000 (NULL)               0x0
```
Note that `STRTAB` and `SYMTAB` should point to different objects (more specifically, different sections) but in this case they point to the same address: the string table (.dynstr). This does not cause an issue during execution because there are no symbols to resolve.

There is a branch `wip/sizzle` that improves windows support. However, it's very complicated and still not very good.

We currently don't implement any syscalls (SSDT entries) for windows, and even on wip/sizzle we only implement a small number of kernel32.dll simprocedures.

On top of that, most of our static analyses will not work because they assume caller-cleanup calling conventions.

Apart from using the above mentioned callee cleanup finder, there is no way for angr to automatically determine the correct calling convention when stubbing external functions with simprocedures. Since each library defines its own calling convention, it is not correct for angr to assume stdcall for all windows x86 dlls. Instead, each [simlibrary](https://github.com/angr/angr/blob/master/angr/procedures/definitions/__init__.py) can specify the calling convention for the corresponding dll that it implements [[example](https://github.com/angr/angr/blob/bef5cb727f7d88c8513f73b7a4b861ce4ae340ea/angr/procedures/definitions/kernel32.py#L8)]. As long as your simprocedure has the correct number of arguments and is defined as callee-cleanup, angr will effectively emulate a `ret X` upon completion of the simprocedure, where X corresponds to the number of arguments. See [here](https://github.com/angr/angr/blob/922348c4989d66b437a6b710f9ce2f9ce2d4aec0/angr/calling_conventions.py#L537-L569) for the actual implementation.

It's a little hard to follow your example, but you have to remember that C code is, in the end, just an abstraction over assembly code, and angr's analysis runs on assembly code. "uninitialized" means that there is no known value for the memory when you read from it. By default in angr, all reads from uninitialized values return unconstrained symbolic variables.

So, if you have a pointer to an array and you use it to access a[-1], the result will depend on the memory layout of the program. If something else is using the memory pointed to by &a[-1], then the read will return that value, or if a previous function call used that region of memory as part of its stack frame, then you'll get back whatever value was written there previously. However, if nothing has ever been written to that address before, you'll get back a symbolic value. This makes sense because when you're analyzing a program with incomplete state, like in your example where you start execution from the start of the function, it's possible that the value at that address could be user-controlled from a previous function call.

By default `Function`s has their own block cache in `Function._block_cache`. When you are iterating in `Function.blocks`, it calls `Function._get_block()`, which results in all blocks being cached in `_block_cache`. I think your issue can be resolved by disabling the `_block_cache`: comment out this line: [`self._block_cache[addr] = block`](https://github.com/angr/angr/blob/master/angr/knowledge/function.py#L145).

The tricky part with the `generate_presigned_url()` is that it is a general method that is applied to all services, but it is really up to the service in whether they actually support using presigned url's and this decision differs from service to service. Given that I got a serialization error back, it looks like step functions does not support using presigned url's.

Instead, I think to support the general functionality of generating/signing a request to defer making the HTTP request for later, we will need to expose a `generate_presigned_request()`, but for now I think you will have to use the `client.describe_execution()` method. Marking as a feature request for the missing ``load()`` method. This looks like one of the gaps in the resource model where the create call does not completely fill up the properties of the multipart upload. However, a ``load()`` method was never attached to the model mainly because in order to get all of the data related to a single multipart upload, all of the multipart uploads need to be listed, which could potentially take a lot of requests to the API to load that single object. If we can figure out a way to avoid that, it would be good to add. Otherwise, we would need to determine if it is acceptable for potentially many API requests to be made for a single resource load.

As to the misspelling, that is a [bug](https://github.com/boto/boto3/issues/916) that we need to figure out a fix for in a backwards compatible way.
One alternative is to move it up into `generate_presigned_url`. That would eliminate the need for a handler as well as the need for adding to the context.

My implementation works and isn't for 'production', so don't feel any obligation to spend time/effort on it...
The application is creating an input file for a dispersal estimation program (which I've got working, but am still trying to figure out how to (or if I can) get useful information out of.)

I'm starting with a VCF (converted to hdf5) filtered for accessible sites and snpEff MODIFIER (also doing those filters in `allel` would be good for production work).
1. take a specific subset of samples.
2. biallelic
3. non-singleton
4. (the odd part) minor allele (could be ref or alt) occurs in exactly N *samples*

The code based on the PCA and Fst examples from your blog:
```
    # load the callset
    callset = h5py.File(args.callset, mode='r')
    all_sample_ids = [ _.decode('utf-8') for _ in callset[list(callset.keys())[0]]['samples'] ]
    # convert the callset to a genotype array (@TCC single chrom version for now)
    g_all = allel.GenotypeChunkedArray(callset[chrom]['calldata']['genotype'])

    ## Subset by sample
    sample_idxs = samplenames2idxs(callset[chrom], sample_ids)
    print("sample_idxs:", sample_idxs)
    if not all(sample_idxs[i] <= sample_idxs[i+1] for i in range(len(sample_idxs)-1)):
        print("ERROR: sample_ids must be in same order as in the callset file!", file=sys.stderr)
    else:
        if sample_ids != [ _.decode('utf-8') for _ in callset[list(callset.keys())[0]]['samples'] ]:
            g = g_all.subset(None, sample_idxs)

    ## Filter

    # allele counts at each variant
    ac = g.count_alleles()

    # allele type counts (just for curiosity)
    multi_allelic = np.count_nonzero(ac.max_allele() > 1)
    biallelic = np.count_nonzero(ac.max_allele() == 1)
    print('input sites:', g.n_variants)
    print('multi/biallelic:',multi_allelic,'/',biallelic,'=',(multi_allelic/biallelic))
    print('singletons:',np.count_nonzero((ac.max_allele() == 1) & (ac[:, :2].min(axis=1) <= 1)))

    # filtering (biallelic)
    flt1 = (ac.max_allele() == 1)
    flt1 = np.logical_and(flt1, ac[:, :2].min(axis=1) > 1)
    print('biallelic filter passes:',sum(flt1))

    # filter by number of *samples* which have a low-freq allele (either hom or het)
    # still using ac
    nref = g.to_n_ref(fill=-1)[:] # @TCC the '[:]' converts from ChunkedArray to numpy.array, possibly not needed
    nsamp_alt = (nref > 0).sum(axis=1)
    nsamp_ref = np.sum(np.logical_and(nref >= 0, nref < 2), axis=1)
    flt2 = np.logical_or(nsamp_ref == TARGET_OBSERVATION_COUNT, nsamp_alt == TARGET_OBSERVATION_COUNT)
    print('variants with', TARGET_OBSERVATION_COUNT,
          'samples obsered to have non-major allele:', sum(flt2))

    # combine the filters
    flt = np.logical_and(flt1, flt2)
    # apply filter (Don't forget to filter the positions/varants too!)
    print('combined filter passes:', sum(flt))
    gf = g.compress(flt, axis=0)
    pos_all = allel.SortedIndex(callset[chrom]['variants']['POS'][:])
    posf = pos_all.compress(flt)

    # should have same num of positions and rows in genotype array
    print(posf.shape, "should have same positions as", gf.shape)
```

The default handlers implement the default logging (which actually predates handler support).  These loggers by default log to a logging.NullHandler which eats the log messages unless you've configured the 'backoff' logger to do something else with them.  At least at the time my understanding was this was considered the best practice for implementing logging in a library.

When we implemented the `on_backoff`, `on_success`, and `on_giveup` handler support, it was too tempting to reimplement the loggers as handlers themselves.  The idea was you'd always want that default logging to happen, because if you didn't you could simply leave the 'backoff' logger unconfigured.  It's a bit of an odd twist of logic in that code snippet you quoted, but that was the idea behind it.

We'd have to be careful changing that behavior because some people may be relying on it.  I'm curious what your use case is?  If you are trying to customize logging behavior, you may want to log to your own logger and leave the 'backoff' logger configured to its NullHandler.

Note that the cleanup isn't actually conditional on whether or not the function had to create its own `TemporaryDirectory` instance - the function always calls `requirements_dir.cleanup()`, even if the requirements directory was passed in from outside.

So even though this is definitely a use case that `ExitStack` was designed to handle (via `ExitStack.callback`), given that `pipenv` supports Python 2.7 and doesn't otherwise depend on `contextlib2`, I think a custom context manager would be a better fit for the situation:

    @contextlib.contextmanager
    def call_cleanup(obj):
        try:
            yield obj
         finally:
             obj.cleanup()

Alternatively, if we can rely on the passed in `requirements_dir` object always being a context manager in its own right (the same way that `pipenv._compat.TemporaryDirectory` is), then there isn't even a need for a wrapper, we can just write:

    with requirements_dir:
        ...

This introduces an additional environment variable `PIPENV_SHELL`. If set, shell detection is skipped completely. The old `SHELL` introspection is kept as a fallback if detection fails.

A run down of how shell detection works now:

1. If `PIPENV_SHELL` is set, use it.
2. Detect surrounding shell…
    1. (Only on POSIX) The surrounding shell is the login shell. Use the value of `SHELL`.
    2. Use the detected shell executable.
3. Detection failed. Check if `SHELL` is set; if so, use it.
4. Prompt the user to set `PIPENV_SHELL` explicitly.

This now calls `dist.activate(replace=True)` for runtime-added dists (same as previous behavior), only modifying the activation of the initial set, loading with `replace=False`, so that sys.path is left completely unmodified for normal behavior.

I'm still not confident about what circumstances there are in which packages are activated beyond the initial set, so this may not be doing the right thing in every case. It seems to me that `sys.path` should not be modified in any way, unless:

1. the package is certainly not on sys.path
2. there are known conflicts with other packages already on sys.path

This PR does not go that far, but it would be nice if ultimately pkg_resources modification of sys.path could be confined to clearly defined, explicit, rare cases.

I've done some investigating of the feasibility of implementing a lock for the bcolz carray public methods. In principle I think the appropriate lock in general would be a read-write lock, i.e., single-writer multiple-reader. Commit 911c763 has a RWLock implementation borrowed from django, and uses the lock when entering public methods like ``__getitem__()``, ``__setitem__()``, ``append()``, etc.

I'd really appreciate some thoughts on whether this is worth pursuing. There are a couple of issues that make this less than straightforward, so making the full bcolz public API thread-safe would take some further work. In particular, I could not immediately see how to make thread-safe implementations of any method that returns an iterator, e.g., ``__iter__()`` or ``where()``. Partly I think this is because a carray is allowed to be its own iterator, but even if that were not the case it's not obvious to me how to handle iterators. 

FWIW the test suite runs with bcolz in thread-safe mode, i.e., there are no situations that result in a deadlock. However a number of tests fail because I am currently raising NotImplementedError for public methods that are not implemented in thread-safe mode.

It certainly would be an easier option to require that multithreaded libraries like dask implement their own write lock. It's tempting to go for that. Bcolz could then just release the GIL around blosc calls, and leave synchronisation up to the caller. 

Btw I'm not invested in either approach so very open to discussion. This has been a great learning exercise so not wasted effort either way.

Good example!

`get_default_state` will automagically create these quantities in the state, since this method will put the tracers as new inputs on components.

The reason I would put these methods into a `TracerPacker` is separation of concerns - this is a really nice case of composition. It's similar to why I have separate classes/objects that handle validation of each of the types of properties, instead of putting them as methods on the base classes. This is a clear responsibility with a specific amount of data required to carry it out, and works well as a new object. This could be used only internally in the base classes and never exposed to the user, potentially within helper methods as you're asking for (especially if we want to allow external writers to customize the packing and unpacking methods).

My current understanding is that a dynamics component would never both require a quantity as an input and use it as a tracer. If that's the case, we should add a new property flag that would indicate this (maybe `tracer` which by default is `False`). By default, quantities present in inputs (not indicated as tracers) that are registered as tracers would not be packed into the tracer array for the object that has it as an input.

> If you are suggesting that we allow conda install or conda update to cause an unsatisfiable environment without the use of a --force command

No, certainly not.  I think you answered my question during Demo Friday when you said that attempts at consistency are limited to a current atomic `conda` command.  My one clarifying question on this in the way of a scenario:

Universe of packages:

    A.1 dep: X>=1, Y=1
    B.1 dep: X=2, Y=1
    B.2 dep: X=2, Y=2
    X.[123] dep: None
    Y.[123] dep: None

Initially empty system.

    conda install A

-> will install `A.1` (assuming it is the latest), and in turn this will result in `X.3` and `Y.1` being installed as dependencies (both installed for the first time, take the latest that satisfies the dependencies)

-> same behavior "today" as this proposed change.

    conda install B

-> today, I think this will install `B.2`, since it is the latest, and its dependencies `X.2` (a downgrade) and `Y.2` (an upgrade).  The user gets the latest version of `B` which might be (I'd say "probably is") what they wanted.  If `A.1` "accidentally" works with `Y.2` (more recent than what it specifies as its actual dependencies), lucky you! But if not, `A` has silently been broken as a result of the installation of `B` (which in no way refers to `A`, and there is no reason to think a user would be aware that `X` and `Y` were deps of `A`).

-> with these changes, IIUC, this will install `B.1` and downgrade `X` to `X.2`, since doing so satisfies the user's request (which didn't specify any particular version for `B`) and the downgrade of `X` from `X.3` to `X.2` continues to satisfy `A.1`'s dependencies.  Will the user be told that a newer `B.2` version was skipped?  Because if they aren't, they might be confused that the blog post they just read about the cool new features of `B` don't work for them when they `conda install` it. (maybe because they "naturally" expect this, or because they've been using conda for 3 years, and that is the behavior they've always experienced).

    conda install Y

-> today and with the proposed changes the user would be told *"Package `Y.1` is already installed".  Interestingly if the change happens where we know about packages that were explicitly requested vs. those that were only installed as dependencies, I suppose this action would need to now "mark" `Y` as an "explicitly requested" package.

-> today you don't get any information that there is a `Y.3` available, though often that is exactly what the user was wanting: the latest version of Y.

-> with the proposed changes, **perhaps** there would be scope to say something sensible like *"Z.1 installed, Z.4 latest available version that satisfies other packages' dependencies (upgrade possible), Z.6 latest but breaks dependencies"*

    conda update Y

-> today, `Y.3` gets installed and the user goes and tries it out and is happy that they have the latest `Y`.  But what they didn't know is that `A` and `B` might now be broken, and certainly their stated dependencies are no longer satisfied.

-> with these changes, IIUC, conda will report *"Package `Y.1` already installed"*, and same comment above about some additional hint that potentially could be provided: *"Y.1 installed, Y.1 latest available version that satisfies other packages' dependencies, Y.3 latest but breaks dependencies"*

    conda install X==1

-> today, `X.1` is installed.  `A`'s dependencies are satisfied (well, other than the `conda update Y` from the last command that moved it to `Y.3`, but you get the point), but `B`s dependencies are broken.

-> with these changes the user is told *"Unsatisfiable package constraints: installing `X` will break dependencies of packages already installed `[B]`"*

    conda install --force X==1

-> today, `--force` forces the installation of the specified packages, even if they are already installed, they are just re-installed.  There is also `--no-deps` which installs a package (or set of explicitly specified package names, possibly with version specifiers) but none of its (their) dependencies.  IMPORTANT: `--no-deps` is implied by `--force`

-> with the changes you're proposing `--force` will have different meaning I think, and I believe there are several levels at which one might want to `--force`:

1. People will still want to force-install packages as a reinstall (same version). Unclear if in this new system that capability should or shouldn't trigger dependency re-installation.

2. People will want to have a `conda` command line that "does stuff", and they just want that stuff to happen, triggering any actions related to dependencies, and not caring if it violates package dependencies of things **not** specified on the command line.  Basically this is partly what "today's" conda does without any special flags.

-> as a footnote there is *also* the concept of `--maximal` or `--minimal` which say *"update as [many/few] packages as possible in satisfying this particular operation"*, captured today in the `--update-deps` and `--no-update-deps` flags.

Just because a `conda update` triggered a problem for you does not strictly lead to it being a fact that there is a problem with any of the newly installed conda packages. It just means that the amalgamation of that new software and your existing system environment are now not compatible.

In other words it can mean that an updated DLL has become incompatible with a DLL loaded from outside of the `Anaconda Distribution` due to DLL-hell.

How did you install `Anaconda`? `Just Me` or `All Users`? Did you elect to add it `PATH` or not?

How do you launch `scipy`? Do you use the `Anaconda Prompt`? Which OS are you even using? Your prompt beginning `$>` does not *look* like Windows. You haven't shown either `conda info` nor `conda list --show-channel-urls` nor the value of `$PATH`/`%PATH%. Without these details we can only guess at what's going on.

.. but my main point is that contrary to your suggestion:

> Maybe conda needs to test crucial packages better before releasing?

.. I can guarantee that we *do* test our software *extensively*, each and every package.

model = AModel()
gates = MockGates('gates', model=model)

meter = MockMeter('meter', model=model)

# The station is a container for all instruments that makes it easy

# it's nice to have the key parameters be part of the global namespace

c0, c1, c2, vsd = gates.chan0, gates.chan1, gates.chan2, source.amplitude

Good that you solved the unused lines. On my side there is still some weird behaviour of namespaces/prefixes. Made a small more reproducible example. Let's solve one issue at a time and focus on the duplicate prefix 'pn80'. The unwanted prefix 'pn80' is created when a new namespaces overrides the current prefix.

This is result of replacing namespace:
```
@prefix n80: <_NotANamespace> .
@prefix pn80: <http://www.opengis.net/ont/geosparql#> .
@prefix rdf: <http://www.w3.org/1999/02/22-rdf-syntax-ns#> .
@prefix rdfs: <http://www.w3.org/2000/01/rdf-schema#> .
@prefix xml: <http://www.w3.org/XML/1998/namespace> .
@prefix xsd: <http://www.w3.org/2001/XMLSchema#> .

pn80:Geometry a <http://www.w3.org/2002/07/owl#Class> ;
    rdfs:isDefinedBy <http://www.opengis.net/ont/geosparql#/test> .
```

This is preferred outcome:
```
@prefix n80: <_NotANamespace> .
@prefix rdf: <http://www.w3.org/1999/02/22-rdf-syntax-ns#> .
@prefix rdfs: <http://www.w3.org/2000/01/rdf-schema#> .
@prefix xml: <http://www.w3.org/XML/1998/namespace> .
@prefix xsd: <http://www.w3.org/2001/XMLSchema#> .

n80:Geometry a <http://www.w3.org/2002/07/owl#Class> ;
    rdfs:isDefinedBy <http://www.opengis.net/ont/geosparql#/test> .
```

This is the small reproducible script that creates the bug:
```
from rdflib import URIRef, Namespace, Graph
from rdflib.namespace import RDF, OWL, RDFS

g = Graph()

The flag is a `float` flag, so you need to supply a float default value or a string that can be parsed as a float. For example, use `0.0` as the default:

```python
tf.app.flags.DEFINE_float('input', 0.0, 'input for the model')
```

An empty string is not valid.

Since `absl.flags` can be defined in any module, there isn't an ordering of when they are defined. So we can't *change* them to be positional. Those must be flags, i.e. specified with `--echo=value` or `--echo value`.

However, you will be able to define positional arguments using `argparse`:

```python
parser = argparse_flags.ArgumentParser('An argparse + app.run example')
parser.add_argument('integers', metavar='N', type=int, nargs='+',
                    help='an integer for the accumulator')
```

You will also be able to use the `argparse` sub-parsers. Here is an example:

```python
flags.DEFINE_string('echo', 'Hello', 'Message to echo.')

parser = argparse_flags.ArgumentParser(description='Sub parser example.',)
subparsers = parser.add_subparsers(help='The command to execute.')

roll_dice_parser = subparsers.add_parser(
    'roll_dice', help='Roll a dice.',
    # Do not make absl FLAGS available after the subcommand `roll_dice`.
    inherited_absl_flags=None)
roll_dice_parser.add_argument('--num_faces', type=int, default=6)

shuffle_parser = subparsers.add_parser('shuffle', help='Shuffle inputs.')
shuffle_parser.add_argument(
    'inputs', metavar='I', nargs='+', help='Inputs to shuffle.')
```

By default, `absl.flags` can be specified before or after the positional sub-command. Use `inherited_absl_flags=None` to only allow *before* (the `roll_dice` sub-command in the example).

Usage:
```bash
./program --echo=value roll_dice --num_faces 20
./program shuffle 1 2 3 4 --echo=value
```

If you execute 'export MXNET_TEST_SEED=42' before running your tests, then every test (except those that override the seed like the 1234 case below) with have the seeds set to 42. If you instead execute 'export MXNET_MODULE_SEED=42' then a random number generator seeded by 42 will provide the sequence of RNG seeds for the tests of each module (i.e. file). If your tests pass in this mode, they will do so repeatedly since every test's seed is deterministic. 

I'm hoping that most people will want to run with no environment variables set. Yes, that means that the tests will get different seeds each time, but those seeds are known and printed out for any test that throws an exception. Even a core-dump can be reproduced in isolation by rerunning the module first with the same seed in a debug mode. So far, this methodology has made it easy for me to track down and fix many of our test's random failures. Most often this has been a case of too-small tolerances or issues with use of the finite difference method, but not always! I'm hoping this PR will be incorporated and will lead to a stronger framework.

It's always the stupid file permissions!

I would like to share that I too was experiencing the "App not loaded"  issue, I had everything in place, it took me testing almost every configuration I could think of and a ton of frustration. In the end it turned out to be that because I was using file logging, the permissions on the log file were blocking the celery user I had setup. Strange that it was causing this apps error, but once I updated the file permissions everything worked. 

Here's how I eventually figured it out (this may help someone else debug): 
I sudo'd in as my celery user (everything seemed to work fine as me, the owner and root)
Tried and ran the `multi start...` failed (obviously)
So, just using a single worker, I tried to load celery: `/opt/myapp/.tox/py35/bin/celery worker -A myapp` That failed.
I then activated the virtual environment and ran `celery worker -A myapp` Failed. 
Next I ran `python manage.py check` Hey I got an error about the log file permissions.
Updated the file permissions, and then ran this in reverse, `manage.py check` worked.
Within the virtual environment, worked! 
Deactivated,worked!
Multi, worked! 
Logged out, ran my service, it now worked!

Hoping this may help someone else.

I believe that happens because requests in Ubuntu can't vendor in urllib3 (see [here](https://github.com/kennethreitz/requests/blob/master/requests/packages/__init__.py#L2-L5)), so we had to construct a fallback that uses the version installed on the system instead.

Because the version of urllib3 that's available on Trusty is so old, docker-py's attempt at monkey-patching it is unfortunately failing. Installing a more recent version of urllib3 using `pip` may solve the issue. (`pip install -U urllib3==1.14`)

Adding an option for max_length at this time would not be appropriate; there is currently nothing to configure when using Fire, and 'max_length' isn't important enough to justify adding the first configuration. We will want to add configurations later; we should start a list of potentially configurable things before adding any such configurations though. 3. Currently we don't parse docstrings at all. If we do start parsing docstrings, we'll need to choose a set of standards that we support. Is there a good list of standards? At google we do:

```
Args:
  name: description of argument
Returns:
  Description of return values.
```

Since there are so many docstring standards, I'm hesitant to enter the business of promoting one over the other. I much prefer the approach of presenting the docstring to the user wholesale.
That said, we can definitely improve the UI, such as by removing the label "Docstring:".

[Maybe there is a way to cover a lot of docstring formats at once, e.g. by looking for lines for which the first token is an argument name and using the remainder of that line as the argument description. I don't know how robust something like this would be.]

Thank you for using sentencepiece.

Yes, you can simply feed Chinese and English only text as follows.

`% spm_train --input=en.txt,zh.txt  --model_prefix=shared.model ..`

As spm_train only loads first 10M lines (can be changed --input_sentence_size), and the seed vocabs are generated from the first 2M sentences (--mining_sentence_size), it would be better to randomly merge the files in advance.

```
% shuf en.zh zh.txt | head -1000000 > shared.txt
% spm_train --input=shared.txt --model_prefix=..
```

**Background:**
By default, SentencePiece uses the Unicode script type as a boundary constraint, i.e., pieces crossing different script types will never be extracted.  As Chinese character Kanji and Latin character have different script types, sentencepiece always puts a boundary between, "hockey" "羽毛" (Chinese and English boundary).  --split_by_unicode_script=false disables this feature and may allow to extract the piece like "key羽".

Thank you.

It was a bit hard to read this since there is no formatting, but it looks like the error is that pyspotify can't find libspotify. The libspotify library has to be placed in a directory that is in your library path. This includes `/lib`, `/usr/lib` and the directories listed in `/etc/ld.so.conf`. You have probably placed libspotify in another directory. Note that `/usr/local/lib` is not on the library path in all distributions.

To check which directories and libraries are found, run `ldconfig -v`. Move libspotify to one of the directories listed there, or add the directory you have put it in to `/etc/ld.so.conf` and run `ldconfig`.

This is not a bug with Mopidy-Spotify, so I'm closing this issue. As per our [issue guidelines](https://docs.mopidy.com/en/latest/contributing/#issue-guidelines), please post to https://discuss.mopidy.com/ if you need further help.

In regards to the scipy interface tests not failing previously with long double inputs (I think you may have already figured this out):

`scipy.fftpack.*` will raise errors on long-double input, but the lines below explicitly cast these inputs down to double precision prior to calling scipy routines so that no error will be raised.  (This conversion is done for `numpy.fft.*` as well, although at least on recent numpy this does not seem to be necessary.  I can call np.fft.fft with float128 input and it runs just fine, silently converting to double precision.)
https://github.com/pyFFTW/pyFFTW/blob/ad23f73defdb2ed27c834cadeff2fd772953a8a6/test/test_pyfftw_numpy_interface.py#L223-L227

The long double precision inputs are still used in the FFTW interfaces.  If we just remove the long double test cases as you have done in 135ece5 , then we would not be testing our scipy FFTW interfaces for those dtypes.  I think we want to continue allowing the testing of these precisions in the interfaces even though the comparison to the underlying scipy.fftpack routines is actually based on double-precision.
