It isn't so much an issue as it is a feature request. When integrating with different wrapping tools you often have functions that take a `PyObject*` argument (to a pybind11 wrapped type). What is needed is a convenient way of extracting the `shared_ptr<MyType>` from it. And, in the other direction, to create a `pybind11::object` from a `shared_ptr<MyType>` and get a `PyObject*` from it to give back to Python through the other wrapper tool (say when a SWIG function has to return a new instance of a pybind11 wrapped type).

The basic approach I would be to use copy out the PYBIND11_TYPE_CASTER macro yourself, but change the `value` member to a `std::unique_ptr<YourType>`.  Then in `load()` you would do `value.reset(new YourType(...))`.

It depends on what you want to identify. InstanceUUID means the VM. Then there's the BIOS UUID which identifies the Guest OS at the BIOS level. Hypothetically a guest OS Bios can be lifted out of a VM instance then moved to another VM instance which has a new UUID. This kind of identity swapping can happen during some VM copy, clone, or movement operations.

In other situations, a name value or a MOR ID (aka moref) is only valid within the context of a single vCenter and for many practical situations this is unique enough. In others, it's woefully inadequate to identify a virtual machine by because there are a very large number of vCenters and virtual machines ... and there's no guarantee that moref number 5 is the same conceptual machine on vCenter A and vCenter B ... in fact its almost universally not the same.

My opinion is that exposing the MOR ID and the name as identifiers on the API was a mistake. Many people have good reasons to *disagree* with that opinion. I have no problem with them being understood as descriptions ... you can use the name or moref id as a field to find a managed object in a pile of managed objects just as you could use a bit of description text to find a web page in a search engine. But the value "phil's home page" describes the page living at the canonical URL that identifies Phil's homepage on the internet.

Does that make sense? You can find a page by its title, but you can't navigate to a page by putting its title in the navigation bar of your browser except by special circumstance or coincidence. A VM name is like a page title, a morefid is like a path under a URL.

@gi0baro @niphlod 
1. I am aware of the difference but it is irrelevant for this issue. My example above will produce the exact same result no matter whether Thread B uses `db().update()` or `row.update_record()` on some previously select()ed row. Also, the main concern in your example are rounding errors and programmer mistake. Acquiring a `FOR UPDATE` lock on the record will ensure that concurrent threads cannot modify `account.amount` in the database until the end of transaction.
2. There is no risk of introducing new deadlocks. `SELECT ... FOR UPDATE` and `UPDATE ...` both apply the exact same type of lock on the exact same set of rows. If using `SELECT ... FOR UPDATE` in `Table.update_or_insert()` would cause deadlock, the current implementation will deadlock anyway when control flow reaches the subsequent `update_record()`. UPSERT in PostgreSQL is merely a performance optimization.
3. If you skip the initial `SELECT` and start with `UPDATE` instead, you won't be able to limit it to just one record. At least not with the limited `update()` features that PyDAL currently supports.

Timing the backward pass is a little tricky, but doable as well. Something like this should work (based on [a mailing list post of mine](https://groups.google.com/d/msg/lasagne-users/t_rMTLAtpZo/P_rGw85QEgAJ)):

```python
import sys
import numpy as np
import theano
import theano.tensor as T
from theano.sandbox.cuda import as_cuda_ndarray_variable
import lasagne

scale_factor = map(int, sys.argv[1].split('x'))
input_shape = (64, 3, 32, 32)
layer = lasagne.layers.Upscale2DLayer(input_shape, scale_factor)
output_shape = layer.output_shape

x = T.tensor4()
y = layer.get_output_for(x)
gy = T.tensor4()
gx = theano.grad(None, wrt=x, known_grads={y: gy})

X = np.random.randn(*input_shape).astype(theano.config.floatX)
Y = np.random.randn(*output_shape).astype(theano.config.floatX)
fn_fwd = theano.function([], as_cuda_ndarray_variable(y), givens={x: X})
fn_bwd = theano.function([], as_cuda_ndarray_variable(gx), givens={x: X, gy: Y},
                         on_unused_input='ignore')

fn = fn_fwd if sys.argv[2] == 'fwd' else fn_bwd
for _ in range(10):
    fn()
```

Now you can launch this with a scale factor (e.g., 2x2, or 3x3, or 1x5) as the first argument and "fwd" or "bwd" as the second argument, along with the environment variables I've mentioned (or by further instructing the code as Jeffrey suggested). (It's untested, though, let me know if you can't get it to run.)

Alright, actually yes, it's a separate issue from it not working with masking. It's that `binary_cross_entropy_with_logits` can't deal with `-inf`. Looking at the code [here](https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/blob/master/torch/nn/functional.py#L1730), it has the expression `(-input - max_val).exp()`, where our `input` is `-inf` and `max_val` is `inf`. This produces `nan` instead of the desired `0.0`.

This is a bug in how the argument documentation is parsed. The code responsible is here [docs.py](https://github.com/awslabs/aws-shell/blob/master/awsshell/docs.py#L41).
The idea is that the documentation will look like:
```
--argument1 
some description

--argument2
some description
```

So it will take all the text up until the next argument. In this case there is an example containing `--` in the documentation and thus it stops taking documentation once it reaches that point. A simple fix might be to take until the next occurrence of `\n--`. However, there's another bug with this approach. For the last argument there wont be another occurrence of `--` or `\n--` and it will take *all* of the remaining documentation. That being said, I'm not sure what the best approach to fix this is. @jamesls Any ideas?

To renew certificates, SeSeLe only calls ACME-client as administrator has set. If the ACME-server requires listening port 443 (as Letsencrypt currently does), SeSeLe supports automatic actions to release the port, do renews, and re-enable services on port after. This is on renewer host role.

The facility consists into having SeSeLe installed in a renewer host (with port 443 forwarded), and using "clients" profiles for automatic certificate files transfer to other (unprivileged) remote hosts. SeSeLe can also be installed on those other hosts to make easier the services reload automation, or to be them that get renewed certificate files from "central" host.

Let me explain what is the difference with or without "-1": The order (mModel->charToOrderMap[(unsigned char)aBuf[i]]) is index to twochars (sequences) table (with values 0=negative, 1, 2, 3=positive occurence) created from 64 (=SAMPLE_SIZE) the most frequented chars sorted by their occurence probability in descending order. This table is list with first index=0 (standard python behavior). If you omit "-1" you not only cut off the most frequent twochar occurences (exactly SAMPLE_SIZE length) but you make twochars table inconsistent to charmap table. For example: Let 'e' and 'a' are the most frequent letters. 'e' has order=1 in the charmap table and 'a' has order=2. The twochars table therefore has 'ee' "occurence value" (0,1,2 or 3) in the first place in the list (index "0") and 'ea' value has the second place (index "1"). If the line of the source code above gives order=1 for 'e' and order=2 for 'a' then expression i = (last_order\*SAMPLE_SIZE)+order gives i=64\*1+2 for 'ea', and it points somewhere else but not to second item in our twochars table. If you add "-1" to order evaluation then you get i=64*0+1=1 what is correct index value. Voila, we have a solution. It's all. If you don't understand or don't believe me, you can simple add "-1" to your code and run some test, and if language models are correct you will see the difference.

The tuple case is a laziness of signature, allowing default_value to be passed positionally or as a kwarg. e.g.

```python
Tuple((1,2,3))
```

and

```python
Tuple(default_value=(1,2,3))
```

are equivalent. It's a bit of a sloppy implementation of what should be

```python
Tuple(default_value=..., **deprecated_metadata)
```

which allows the same positional-or-kwarg specification like any normal Python function.

-1 on this from me.  As a user of traitlets for various random side projects, I'd always rather have to explicitly specify a default value than have the traitlet system try to guess what it thinks I might have meant.  The failure mode when you forget to specify a default is easy to reproduce and straightforward to diagnose from the resulting stack trace.  The failure mode when a traitlet unexpectedly has a default is that a value *somewhere* in your program has a value you didn't mean to supply, which is much likely to cause unexpected behavior that's not easily traceable back to the root cause.

It looks like README.rst contains a non-ASCII character (the "micro" symbol, "µ"), so this problem will arise if the default encoding of your python framework is ASCII rather than utf-8. 1) remove the 'µ' symbol in README.rst before building
2) add the following lines to the top of setup.py
    #!/usr/bin/env python
    # -*- coding: utf-8 -*-

Sorry, that I kept ignoring this PR. Upon inspection, this actually exists already so I'm going to close

```python
account = recurly.Account.get('de25a7e399a1451a9e260892b21f5733')
account
# =><recurly.Account object at 0x10929abd0>
account.subscriptions()
# => [<recurly.Subscription object at 0x1092a8b50>]
```

The reason is that `<subscriptions/>` is a link in the xml:

```xml
<account href="https://bhelx.recurly.com/v2/accounts/de25a7e399a1451a9e260892b21f5733">
  <!-- .... -->
  <subscriptions href="https://bhelx.recurly.com/v2/accounts/de25a7e399a1451a9e260892b21f5733/subscriptions"/>
  <!-- .... -->
</account>
```

The `Resource` class automatically turns these links into what we call `relatiator functions` which are able to pull relations.

What you've stumbled upon is a feature of the Requests library. Given a list, it automatically splits the parameters up. Some webservers accept `...&assignment_ids=123&assignment_ids=456&...` as a list, but Canvas does not. For it to be treated as a list, it must have `[]` appended to the end of the param.

So, my earlier suggestion of the library auto-appending `[]` as-needed should work...

...until we run into stuff like [creating grading standards](https://canvas.instructure.com/doc/api/grading_standards.html#method.grading_standards_api.create) with params like `grading_scheme_entry[][name]` and `grading_scheme_entry[][value]`. It's also worth noting these are order-sensitive.

The way we currently handle kwargs is by flattening the dictionaries into entries that look like `param[key1][key2][etc]`, then sending a single `params` or `data` dictionary with those kwargs to the appropriate Requests get/post/etc method. This breaks down with the `grading_scheme_entry` examples I give above because the keys don't follow 2 important principles of dictionaries:
1) Dictionaries have no order, but the order of these params matters
2) Dictionary keys *must* be unique, but duplicate keys are allowed for this endpoint. For example, the following set of arguments is valid: (I split them up for readability)
```
grading_scheme_entry[][name]=pass
grading_scheme_entry[][value]=50
grading_scheme_entry[][name]=fail
grading_scheme_entry[][value]=0
```

We can probably get around number 1 by using `OrderedDict` from `collections`, but number 2 has thrown me for a loop.

This is not something that we have an influence on.

One day ago `urllib3==1.23` was released, which is required not only by patroni, but also by `requests` module, and the last one has very strict version check: `'urllib3>=1.21.1,<1.23'`.

The only possible solution would be to require absolutely the same versions of `urllib3` as `requests` does, but even this won't work reliably, because urllib3 is required by a bunch of other modules and some of them definitely don't have a strict constraint on upper version boundary (python-etcd and kubernetes are examples of such modules).

The only we can do is to wait for a new release of `requests`.

When creating a project with multiple targets, one should specify the project name:
```toml
[project:someproject]
[someexe]
output_name  = "run"
[somelib]
```

Then, another project may include this:
```toml
[project:myproject]
[myexe]
dependencies = ["someproject.somelibrary", "someproject.otherlibrary"]

[project]
# the project file of this project should define the targets "somelibrary" and "otherlibrary"
url         = "https://github.com/someteam/someproject"
```

If no project name is specified, the corresponding toml file can only be used as external project file, if a name is specified in the "consuming" project (this will also override any name specified in that project), e.g.
```toml
[project:someproject]
url         = "https://github.com/someteam/someproject"
```

regarding your question about why the since query bumps the clock, this documentation is helpful to understand how we synchronize the point in time that you placed your query with the stream of events coming from the operating system:

https://facebook.github.io/watchman/docs/cookies.html

The `clock` command doesn't (by default) use a sync cookie, which is why you don't observe the clock value changing.

As @sid0 said, the contents of the clock string are opaque and subject to change across releases; as you've discovered, we've already revised it once to add more data, and the code in the master branch has a "fat clock" concept to support source control aware queries that is pending documentation (on my list for this week).

With all that in mind, can I ask why you're interested in the contents of the clock string?  What is your higher level goal?  Sharing that will help us suggest how to implement it with watchman.

The only use of the url field so far has been to view the quality control. The output of the quality control analysis is a web-page and this is probably why we have named the field `basic:url`. In our mind this was a bunch of files hosted on our file server that should be linked or opened in an iframe. We probably wanted to make the solution general to reference other files as well. In the beginning we probably had full urls to the files. A lot has changed since the first version of our platform. We have probably later changed the links it to relative urls, with the introduction of the purge function. Finally we even clipped out the path prefix when we made the platform more general. I agree that now the `basic:file:iframe/link` would be a more appropriate structure. You can support the `basic:file:iframe`, remove the `basic:url` field altogether and change the field type in the processes in resolwe-bio. The `basic:url:view` can probably be substituted simply by `basic:file`. Some small changes will be needed in the dynamic directive, also.

I think I have another explanation for the behaviour you observed. 

The data in `inp` clearly comes from a unimodal distribution. The HMM on the other hand is bimodal (`n_components` is 2). As a result, the estimated emission probabilities are distributed between the two components, where if `emissionprob_[0, f]` has a relatively high probability then `emissionprob_[1, f]` tends to be zero. Again this process heavily depends on the initialization which is random.

Here's a couple of runs:
```ipython
In [36]: inp = [[0,1]*100 + [2]*200]

In [37]: MultinomialHMM(n_components=2, n_iter=1000).fit(inp).emissionprob_
Out[37]:
array([[  5.00000001e-01,   4.99999999e-01,   2.96075281e-10],
       [  2.93811078e-22,   2.34890265e-09,   9.99999998e-01]])

In [38]: MultinomialHMM(n_components=2, n_iter=1000).fit(inp).emissionprob_
Out[38]:
array([[  7.24880673e-23,   1.58055473e-09,   9.99999998e-01],
       [  5.00000001e-01,   4.99999999e-01,   2.11231690e-10]])
```

If you try to fit a bimodal HMM to a bimodal sample (e.g. a sample from a known HMM) things tend to get a bit more stable:
```ipython
In [56]: model = MultinomialHMM(n_components=2, n_iter=1000)

In [57]: model.startprob_ = np.full(2, .5)

In [58]: model.transmat_ = np.full((2, 2), .5)

In [59]: model.emissionprob_ = np.array([[.5, .3, .2], [.1, .85, .05]])

In [60]: inp, _Z = model.sample(1000)

In [61]: MultinomialHMM(n_components=2, n_iter=1000).fit(inp).emissionprob_
Out[61]:
array([[ 0.24883943,  0.64340111,  0.10775947],
       [ 0.38345166,  0.50429809,  0.11225024]])

In [62]: MultinomialHMM(n_components=2, n_iter=1000).fit(inp).emissionprob_
Out[62]:
array([[ 0.37920094,  0.49343163,  0.12736743],
       [ 0.25473053,  0.65210614,  0.09316333]])
```

> Are there some 'rule of thumb' on how many training data should I prepare?

I don't think I know any, but in general the more data you have the better.

> Is it sufficient to check `model.monitor_.converged` to check whether the model created has converged?

It should be sufficient, yes. However, we implement a slightly abused notion of convergence here. `converged` is `True` if the maximum number of iterations is reached **or** the increase in log-likelihood is below `tol`.

You can check that the convergence was due to the stabilized log-likelihood using the `monitor_.history` attribute:

```python
truly_converged = monitor.history[1] - monitor.history[0] < monitor.tol
```

I am actually already installing a `requirements.txt` file first with `pip-accel`, then doing `pip install -e .`, just as you describe. However, the problem is that the `requirements.txt` file is not always complete (sometimes even empty) or up to date, and in those cases (e.g. when starting a new project from a template), all dependencies or all outdated dependencies will be installed by `pip`, slowly.

Usually, this won't be a big deal. One person will create a project and install unpinned dependencies once, then pin them. But it means we have to make sure that everyone on the team who ever makes a project from the template remembers to pin dependencies sooner than later, and it otherwise wouldn't normally happen until the project was closer to an initial release.

But `pip-accel` is still definitely useful when the `requirements.txt` file is up to date.

opening "the first serial device" (e.g. with `Serial(0)`) just makes no sense anymore where internal serial ports are barely used and USB-serial adapters are common. The old way of assigning a number also did not include all devices (on some OS) or it was confusing (0->COM1).

To enumerate ports, it is better to use [`serial.tools.list_ports`](https://pyserial.readthedocs.org/en/latest/tools.html#module-serial.tools.list_ports). To open a connection to a specific device it may also be easier to use [`serial_for_url`](https://pyserial.readthedocs.org/en/latest/url_handlers.html) with `hwgrep://` especially if it is a USB device which can be identified by its name or VID:PID combination.

Note that both the `h5` and `.asc` files used in this comparison break some valid assumptions that can be made of the data and are used in `NeuroM`:

*  The `h5` does not have real duplicate points on neurite section-section boundaries. It duplicates (x, y, z), but not necessarily d. The assumption is that duplicate points have equal (x, y, z, d). This is now formally specified in the [hbp h5 spec](https://developer.humanbrainproject.eu/docs/projects/morphology-documentation/0.0.2/h5v1.html#file-format)
* The `.asc` file adds similar "duplicate" points at neurite section-section boundaries. These do not appear on "standard" `.asc` files, and seem to be an artifact of some kind of "repair" process or otherwise performed by a "MUK". This breaks the assumption that there are no duplicated points in section-section boundaries. Note that this mangling process is also BBP-specific and ill-defined.

For these reasons, I am reverting the duplicate removal recently introduced for NL `.asc` files. See PR #360. If and when the "duplicate" insertion process is formalized, we may consider making `NeuroM` aware of it.

i made auth.py
```
from functools import wraps
from flask import request, Response, g
from app import jsonrpc
from flask_jsonrpc.exceptions import InvalidCredentialsError

def check_auth(username, password):
    """This function is called to check if a username /
    password combination is valid.
    """
    return username == 'admin' and password == 'admin'

def requires_rpc_auth(f):
    @wraps(f)
    def decorated(*args, **kwargs):
        auth = request.authorization
        if not auth or not check_auth(auth.username, auth.password):
            raise InvalidCredentialsError()
        g.auth = auth
        return f(*args, **kwargs)
    return decorated
```

Makes sense. The convention in Python is that end-of-line comments are associated with the code on that line, and lines that only contain a comment are associated with the following line. So it might be a helpful utility to expand a node's token range to include any end-of-line comments AND any preceding comment-only lines (or maybe only preceding comment-only lines until you hit a blank line). And also to include surrounding parentheses.

(One difference from your proposal is if you have an end-of-line comment after the operator `*` -- it seems most natural to let it stay there, rather than be considered part of the following node.)

There was just a discussion of a similar requirement in https://github.com/gristlabs/asttokens/issues/10 -- about finding end-of-line comments after a token.

Also, to make this completely general-purpose, we'd run into a problem with parentheses and function calls. E.g. in `foo(a + b)`, we do NOT want the argument expansion to be `(a + b)`.

## 3. Breaking changes
- The two conversions columns, `EntityToPersonColumn` and `PersonToEntityColumn`, are deprecated.
- Variables such as `quifam` or `idfam` don't exist anymore. The entity management is now an internal business of each entity. 
- The way entities are declared has changes. You can see the new syntax [here](https://github.com/openfisca/openfisca-france/blob/refactor-entities/openfisca_france/entities.py). The goal was also to make it lighter and avoid repetitions.
- Renaming `entity_class`  -> `entity`

Other than this, the changes are **retro-compatible**, through [introspection of the formula signature](https://github.com/openfisca/openfisca-core/blob/refactor-entities/openfisca_core/formulas.py#L457). If the first argument of the formula is `self`, for instance in `def function('self', simulation, period)`, it will be interpreted as an "old-style" formula. This way, we can progressively and opportunistically rewrite formulas without having to all at once.

The former roles and entities formulas are not fully deprecated either. I wanted to do it, but there are many formulas in France that use them, and some of them are very hard to understand. Some treat differently children, based on their rank in the family, which seems wrong as this rank is arbitrary and not necessarily by age.

 Deprecating former roles and entity conversion methods  should probably be the first step of the `openfisca-france` migration.

```python
import telethon
from telethon import tl

import logging
logging.basicConfig(level=logging.DEBUG)

def init_client():
    global client
    client = telethon.TelegramClient(
        'test',
        api_id = 1234,
        api_hash = 'xxx'
    )
    print(client.__version__)
    client.connect()
    print('connected')
    if not client.is_user_authorized():
        login()
    # Listen for updates
    client.add_update_handler(update_handler)

def login():
    number = '+123456789'
    client.send_code_request(number)
    code = input('enter code:')
    res = client.sign_in(phone_number=number, code=code)
    print(res)

def update_handler(update_object):
    print(repr(update_object))
    
def check_connection():
    try:
        state = client.invoke(tl.functions.updates.GetStateRequest())
    except (TimeoutError, ConnectionError):
        print('TimeoutError, try reconnecting')
        try:
            client.reconnect()
            client._set_updates_thread(running=True)
        except OSError as error:
            print('OSError', error)

def ping():
    return client.invoke(tl.functions.PingRequest(telethon.helpers.generate_random_long()))

init_client()
```

`crashsms` [only sends an alert](https://github.com/Supervisor/superlance/blob/5db5ea80bc37d815ce1fcd5fbcbbdd2c2650c03e/superlance/crashsms.py#L23-L25) when it receives the [`PROCESS_STATE_EXITED`](https://github.com/Supervisor/superlance/blob/5db5ea80bc37d815ce1fcd5fbcbbdd2c2650c03e/superlance/crashsms.py#L70) event, meaning the process has entered the `EXITED` state.  When you stop the process with `supervisorctl stop`, it puts the process into the `STOPPED` state.  Try causing the process to exit another way, e.g. by `kill -9 <pid>` or since Supervisor 3.3.0, `supervisorctl signal 9 <processname>`.  To see detailed messages about what events are being sent to event listeners like `crashsms`, set `loglevel=blat` in the `[supervisord]` section of the config file.

@Flaise we have just added the HTTP API endpoint to get unspent conditions, both on server and driver sides. Please note that the new endpoint is only available on **master** branches, and should be part of the 0.9 and 0.2 releases for the server and driver respectively. You are welcome to try out the master branches, and we'll be happy to answer your questions if you have any.

The driver documentation of the endpoint is [here](https://docs.bigchaindb.com/projects/py-driver/en/master/libref.html#bigchaindb_driver.driver.UnspentsEndpoint.get)

We have yet to write some examples. In the meantime, the above linked documentation contains a small example I will reproduce here for convenience:

> Given a transaction with _id_ `da1b64a907ba54` having an _ed25519_ condition (at index `0`) with `alice`’s public key:
>
> ```python
> >>> bdb = BigchainDB()
> >>> bdb.unspents.get(alice_pubkey)
> ... ['../transactions/da1b64a907ba54/conditions/0']
> ```

The server docs for `GET /unspents` are [here](https://docs.bigchaindb.com/projects/server/en/master/drivers-clients/http-client-server-api.html#get-unspents)

Let us know if you need help!

As of right now, PEX_PYTHON_PATH with only one interpreter will effectively accomplish the same thing as PEX_PYTHON.

Hi,

I've tried to reproduce this, with `range(10)` and `-n5` to more easily see what's going on and this behaves mostly fine from what I can observe.  You can see this more clearly when running the tests with `-v` as that shows you which process was running which test.  If you do this you'll observe that **most** of the tests which run after the blocking happened are all from the same gateway, actually from gw0 since the `x == 0` condition will make sure it's always gw0 which blocks.  In fact when running with only `range(10)` **all** of the tests after the blocking are fro gw0, the *most* part is another scheduler corner case.

This is entirely expected with how the scheduler works currently.  It asks all the processes to collect the tests and then gives batches of the tests to each process.  It waits for the processes to return the results.  So in the `range(10)` `-n5` case the batch gw0 gets is `x=0`, `x=1`.  The master process has no idea that the first one will block, so gw0 starts executing this and can not report back to the master until the entire batch is done.  And when the results of that batch finally come in the suite is finished.

The reason for this batching behaviour is that it is way to expensive, usually, to ask each process to run one test at a time.  So this compromise of the default LoadScheduler is usually reasonable.  Though if there's a good reason this is not reasonable in your case you can write a new scheduler for your usecase in a plugin I believe.  There may be other features in schedulers like marking specific tests which I've currently forgotten.  Basically you'll want to dig into the schedulers if you want to optimise your usecase here.

In light of this I'm going to close this as "working as intended" for now.

It looks like the environment is trying to execute this module runs on GPU.
Most text modules currently available perform string preprocessing
(tokenization, n-gramming) as part of the module. These operation are
usually either not GPU-friendly or outright not compatible with execution
on GPU. It seems that is what is going on here. What happens if you connect
to an environment that runs CPU?

Lattice models are not supported by tf-serving by default. If you want to use lattice models in tensorflow serving, you will need to build tensorflow serving's model_server binary with our C++ kernels. Here's one way to do this.

Step 1) Clone TensorFlow serving git repo.

Step 2) Add additional dependencies to tensorflow_model_server cc binary rule in tensorflow_serving/model_servers/BUILD something like
`@org_tensorflow_lattice//tensorflow_lattice:tensorflow_lattice_cc`

Step 3) Now we need to let bazel know where to find @org_tensorflow_lattice. 
Firstly, let us create a macro in serving/repo.bzl

```
tensorflow_http_archive = repository_rule(
    implementation=_tensorflow_http_archive,
    attrs={
        "git_commit": attr.string(mandatory=True),
        "sha256": attr.string(mandatory=True),
    })

def _tensorflow_lattice_http_archive(ctx):
  git_commit = ctx.attr.git_commit
  sha256 = ctx.attr.sha256

  strip_prefix = "lattice-%s" % git_commit
  # strip_prefix = ""
  urls = [
      "https://mirror.bazel.build/github.com/tensorflow/lattice/archive/%s.tar.gz" % git_commit,
      "https://github.com/tensorflow/lattice/archive/%s.tar.gz" % git_commit,
  ]
  ctx.download_and_extract(
      urls,
      "",
      sha256,
      "",
      strip_prefix)
```

Now let's actually define @org_tensorflow_lattice repo in WORKSPACE using this macro.

```
load("//tensorflow_serving:repo.bzl", "tensorflow_lattice_http_archive")

tensorflow_lattice_http_archive(
    name = "org_tensorflow_lattice",
    sha256 = "f4d8e7a34730ff8b5f896bb213d7a23945af8ec4a7dbc88124658a48eadb9849",
    git_commit = "45336f10ee37326e2e719645d4e1eeb8ce01c523",
)
```

Here git_commit points to the git commit number in the most recent commit number in tensorflow/lattice.git, and sha256 is generated by fetching the code & computing sha256 in my machine. (In fact, tensorflow serving's WORKSPACE explains how to do this for TensorFlow git repo.)

Step 4) Build tensorflow_model_server
```
bazel build -c opt tensorflow_serving/model_servers:tensorflow_model_server
```

Step 5) Use this model server
```
bazel-bin/tensorflow_serving/model_servers/tensorflow_model_server \
  --port=<number you want to use> \
  --model_base_path=<path to your exported model>
```

Note that this is not the only way. As long as you build tensorflow model server linked with our tensorflow_lattice_cc, you're good to go :).

Figured it out: (Need to include a file_version event)
```
from time import sleep

import shutil
import os

from tensorboardX import SummaryWriter
from tensorboardX.src.event_pb2 import SessionLog
from tensorboardX.src.event_pb2 import Event

if os.path.isdir('welp'):
    shutil.rmtree('welp')

val = 3

tensorboard = SummaryWriter(log_dir='welp/train')
tensorboard.file_writer.event_writer.add_event(Event(step=0, file_version='brain.Event:2'))
tensorboard.file_writer.event_writer.add_event(
    Event(session_log=SessionLog(status=SessionLog.START), step=0))
tensorboard.add_scalar('path/one/', 0, 0)
tensorboard.add_scalar('path/two/', 0, 0)
tensorboard.add_scalar('path/one/', val, 1)
tensorboard.add_scalar('path/two/', val, 1)
tensorboard.add_scalar('path/one/', 0, 2)
tensorboard.add_scalar('path/two/', 0, 2)
tensorboard.file_writer.event_writer.add_event(
    Event(session_log=SessionLog(status=SessionLog.STOP), step=2))
tensorboard.close()

sleep(1.0)

tensorboard = SummaryWriter(log_dir='welp/train')
tensorboard.file_writer.event_writer.add_event(Event(step=1, file_version='brain.Event:2'))
tensorboard.file_writer.event_writer.add_event(
    Event(session_log=SessionLog(status=SessionLog.START), step=1))
tensorboard.add_scalar('path/one/', 0, 1)
tensorboard.add_scalar('path/two/', 0, 1)
tensorboard.file_writer.event_writer.add_event(
    Event(session_log=SessionLog(status=SessionLog.STOP), step=1))
tensorboard.close()
```

Update: I've done a little deeper investigation here and have some further insight, and would appreciate some further reporting about your scenarios: see below.

So, the reason our web site has that huge scary warning about avoiding the use of pseudo elements with the SVG with JS method is because, in order to properly figure out what work we need to do to update all the icons on the page—when pseudo elements are enabled—we have to search a huge number of DOM nodes, possibly all of them, multiple times (because there are potentially both `:before` and `:after`) pseudo elements.

It's a sheer problem of DOM size and the fact that—to guarantee correctness—we'd have to search a whole bunch of stuff multiple times. There's no way around that.

Illustration: I took a look on one of my own WordPress sites, looking for the difference in DOM size between having the home page loaded normally, versus having that page open under the Customizer. It's an order of magnitude difference (10x). So I don't think the root cause of this performance problem has to do with the WP Customizer, per se, or whether it makes use of pseudo elements, but rather to do with large DOMs, searching for pseudo elements, and transforming them into icons. Apparently, it just happens to be the case that, when using the WP Customizer, the DOM is an order of magnitude larger _and_ in your scenarios, you have `searchPseudoElements: true`. 

(Granted, it _may_ also be aggravated by something about how the Customizer works, causing the mutation observer to search over a larger portion of that huge DOM than it might otherwise in some other large DOM scenario.)

Some actual numbers from me:
- *422* the number of DOM nodes on my home page, outside of the Customizer (I loaded the page when not logged in, in a Chrome Incognito window).
- *6,369* the number of DOM nodes on the home page when logged in and running the Customizer.

So, 422 vs 6369 is a gigantic difference.

Elsewhere out in the wild:
- 858: Medium.com front page
- 1,992: my Facebook news feed
- 21,327! my twitter.com news feed

You can get those numbers for your own WordPress pages by navigating to them, and then opening the JavaScript console and running the following:
`document.querySelectorAll('*').length`

What we're planning to do is add some throttling so that when the DOM size is greater than some reasonable X, we log a console warning and stop trying to search pseudo elements, so as not to crash the browser. The console warning would alert as to _why_ the icon aren't showing up as expected—because to attempt to do so would probably crash the browser. And then we'd also provide a config option that would allow you to override that X, if you have reason to increase that threshold above X. This is really just taking that huge scary warning on our web site and putting it right in the runtime so it's more clear: _yes, this is what we told you would happen_ 😉 

Now, I need your help to determine what that reasonable default X should be. It clearly needs to be lower than those cases where you all are seeing your browsers crash. 

So, could I hear some of those node count comparisons from your own scenarios, please,
@Kieran-Calvert, @transbetacism, @ccoplestone ? (And anyone else following this, I invite your reports as well.) I'd like to see, for those cases where it's binding up the browser, what's the node count on that page outside of the Customizer, and then what's the count inside the Customizer?

@Ichimonji10 proposed option is better, of course, but the issue is different:
because of [this](https://github.com/SatelliteQE/nailgun/pull/260/files#diff-3822710a997c3f2f7c3e93e884af69caR2036) line we'll always have `self._owner_type == None`, so
```python
    if '_owner_type' in attrs:
        attrs['owner_type'] = attrs.pop('_owner_type')
```
will always create attribute `owner_type` (at least equal to `None`), even if it wasn't defined in initialized entity instance.
As a result, default `Host().update_payload()` will look like this `{'host': {'owner_type': None}}` instead of `{'host': {}}`. Of course, i can fix unit tests and pretend it's supposed to work like that, but that's just wrong way of solving the issue :smile: 
So imo something like this should be applied:
```python
    def get_values(self):
        """Correctly set the ``owner_type`` attribute."""
        attrs = super(Host, self).get_values()
        if '_owner_type' in attrs and attrs['_owner_type'] is not None:
            attrs['owner_type'] = attrs.pop('_owner_type')
        else:
            attrs.pop('_owner_type')
        return attrs
```

This kind of ciphersuite setting is controlled by your webserver (or other
TLS endpoint) configuration, not by the content of the cert.  In almost
all cases, TLS certs don't specify anything about the ciphersuites that
they can (or can't) be used with.  In this case you can disable this
ciphersuite in your httpd.conf (if you're using Apache) -- in any case,
you don't need a new or different cert.

There is a lot that can be said about ciphersuite selection and about
DH groups, but you can find suggestions from Mozilla that LE has decided
to adopt as defaults at

https://wiki.mozilla.org/Security/Server_Side_TLS

But if you really need to work around this in the meantime, there are workarounds:

1. You could also probably just rsync the venv directory `~/.local/share/letsencrypt` from a machine with more RAM.

2 Try [making a swapfile](http://www.cyberciti.biz/faq/linux-add-a-swap-file-howto/) to give yourself more memory.  If your instance is spinning-disk-backed, this may be horribly slow, but SSD should be okay.

I'd suggest doing the cleanup in the "monitor" process.

Currently we have two monitors (I assume down the road we will just have one). @stephanie-wang does GCS cleanup after a driver exits belong in the Python monitor or the C++ monitor?

Currently that functionality is all in the Python monitor, see https://github.com/ray-project/ray/blob/7edc677304f90bcab0a01e9722e59e36dd9afcb0/python/ray/monitor.py#L429-L436 (though note that that function is entirely for legacy Ray).

@elibol was working on an equivalent for x-ray, see #2289, and I think some of that approach is necessary to address this issue.

Basically, the pattern is
1. The driver dies/exits.
2. The node manager broadcasts a message that the driver died.
3. The monitor receives the message and cleans up the GCS entries for that driver.

@zhijunfu thanks, I'd be happy to review a PR for improving lineage allocation and deallocation!

For the serialization/deserialization overheads, I think we can improve it by rethinking some of the lineage stash algorithm. The workload you've been testing with causes actor A's node manager to send the entire stashed lineage of every task submitted to actor B to actor B's node manager. Even though the lineage gets periodically evicted, it's pretty clear that this will be worse than doing nothing at all, like in legacy Ray.

There is a lot of duplicate lineage that's getting sent with the current lineage stash algorithm. I think we can improve it by keeping track of which node managers have received which lineage so far. Then, when node manager A forwards a task for actor B to node manager B, it can send the message without any uncommitted lineage, since it knows that it already sent node manager B the same lineage with a previous message.

Also, from running the script you sent me, I noticed that in xray, actor A only finishes pushing messages to actor B shortly before actor B finishes reading the messages. This is in contrast to legacy ray, where actor A finishes pushing soon (6s) after starting. I believe this is due to a known issue that we've had with asio where, for a single event handler on a socket, we're only handling one message at a time. This means that if there are multiple messages in the socket, the next message will get delayed until its event handler is processed. We should fix this by processing as many messages as possible from any type of socket, possibly with some upper bound on how much time we can spend in a single event handler. I think this will probably help with the slowdown that you're seeing as well.

>> NOTE 2: One other option for intuitive configuration is to have default constant number of brackets and trials.

> This makes sense. This would be an alternative to grid search right?

We can do this instead of time limiting. I think this is orthogonal to grid search; I meant the following: agnostic of number of trials and configurations given, we maintain the same number of brackets (like `brackets=5`) and same number of trials per band (120). Then, given `max_iter`, we can scale the HyperBand configuration appropriately while maintaining the above.

For example, in the example on Kevin Jamieson's blog, he shows the following (with `max_iter=81`):

```
s=4             s=3             s=2             s=1             s=0
n_i   r_i       n_i   r_i       n_i   r_i       n_i   r_i       n_i   r_i
---------       ---------       ---------       ---------       ---------
 81    1         27    3         9     9         6     27        5     81
 27    3         9     9         3     27        2     81
 9     9         3     27        1     81
 3     27        1     81
 1     81
```
I propose that given, for example `max_iter = 810`, we get the following configuration:
```
s=4             s=3             s=2             s=1             s=0
n_i   r_i       n_i   r_i       n_i   r_i       n_i   r_i       n_i   r_i
---------       ---------       ---------       ---------       ---------
 81    10        27    30        9     90        6     270       5     810
 27    30        9     90        3     270       2     810
 9     90        3     270       1     810
 3     270       1     810
 1     810
```
Note that this is a departure from the *real parameters* you should be getting with `max_iter=810`, which would have about 900 raw trials.

Note that this example requires TensorFlow 1.0.0.

I have a fairly rich [cache simulator](https://github.com/ben-manes/caffeine/wiki/Simulator) which focuses on the hit rate curve. In [traces](https://github.com/ben-manes/caffeine/wiki/Efficiency) ARC's performance varies but generally stays close to LRU. You might want to run your own traces to see how your cache might perform using different algorithms.

I would recommend using TinyLFU + FIFO for disk, perhaps with LRU in memory. The reason being that LRU handles sparse bursts very well, like @salsasepp case of workers crunching an item and then never using it again. It does poorly when the cache is polluted by one-hit wonders, e.g. a table scan, so frequency keeps the most valuable items that are otherwise flushed out. Due to disk, you also want to avoid I/O churn and for writes and only retain the frequently used entries (since in-memory absorbed all the recency).

TinyLFU is an admission policy that rejects new items if they have a lower estimated frequency than the eviction policy's victim. It uses a popularity sketch, e.g. CountMin, making it very compact. You could keep the sketch in memory, filter out most of the noisy writes, and have nice sequential I/O due to FIFO. Most likely this would give you better performance and be simpler than the alternatives like ARC or ClockPro.

先说一下常规的请求
==============
浏览器发起http请求, 正常的请求穿过GFW到达服务器, 服务器响应数据

加上shadowsocks的请求
=================
浏览器要配置socks5代理(或者是配置系统socks5代理, 让浏览器走系统代理), 这时候浏览器发起http请求会被改写成socks5请求(估计浏览器内置了 socks5 客户端), 这个 socks5 客户端要先和 shadowsocks local 端进行握手, 客户端先发送握手包, 这个是握手包的每一个字节的解释(来自<https://zh.wikipedia.org/wiki/SOCKS>):
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/13357301/33827005-be46b46e-dea1-11e7-84e1-b1fe0ef823f3.png)
由于 shadowsocks 只支持 socks5 协议, 所以第一个字节必须是`\x05`, 你这里的报错就是出自这里, 第一个字节不是 `\x05`, 贴一下报这个错的 python 代码(来自<https://github.com/shadowsocks/shadowsocks/blob/master/shadowsocks/tcprelay.py#L514>):

```python
def _check_auth_method(self, data):
        # VER, NMETHODS, and at least 1 METHODS # <https://zh.wikipedia.org/wiki/SOCKS#SOCKS5> 维基百科对于 socks5 握手协议包 的每一个字节的解释
        if len(data) < 3:
            logging.warning('method selection header too short')
            raise BadSocksHeader
        socks_version = common.ord(data[0])
        nmethods = common.ord(data[1])
        if socks_version != 5:
            logging.warning('unsupported SOCKS protocol version ' +
                            str(socks_version))
            raise BadSocksHeader
        if nmethods < 1 or len(data) != nmethods + 2:
            logging.warning('NMETHODS and number of METHODS mismatch')
            raise BadSocksHeader
        noauth_exist = False
        for method in data[2:]:
            if common.ord(method) == METHOD_NOAUTH:
                noauth_exist = True
                break
        if not noauth_exist:
            logging.warning('none of SOCKS METHOD\'s '
                            'requested by client is supported')
            raise NoAcceptableMethods
```

怎么处理
======================

因为想要给 shadowsocks local 握手的客户端不是 socks5 客户端, 检查一下你在哪里配置了 shadowsocks local 的地址, 然后看一下这个客户端是不是 socks5 客户端

Parameter `-d` only support the following commands:

- start
- stop
- restart

(from shadowsocks/daemon.py)
```
def daemon_exec(config):
    if 'daemon' in config:
        if os.name != 'posix':
            raise Exception('daemon mode is only supported on Unix')
        command = config['daemon']
        if not command:
            command = 'start'
        pid_file = config['pid-file']
        log_file = config['log-file']
        if command == 'start':
            daemon_start(pid_file, log_file)
        elif command == 'stop':
            daemon_stop(pid_file)
            # always exit after daemon_stop
            sys.exit(0)
        elif command == 'restart':
            daemon_stop(pid_file)
            daemon_start(pid_file, log_file)
        else:
            raise Exception('unsupported daemon command %s' % command)
```

I understand that the RFC is not a standard but why convert (crlf) to (lf) if the user made the choice of (crlf) at first? At least I could understand that csvclean *doesn't* convert (lf) to (crlf) nor (any-type-of-end-of-line) to (any-type-of-end-of-line).

I'm currently writing a document for thousands of futur Open Data producers in France (a new law made Open Data mandatory for public bodies with 3,500+ people). [Document is in french but I can publish a draft here.] I would like to promote csvkit but it's hard to do so until files produced by csvkit get a warning in CSVLint. These people doesn't have big skills with data and command line tools (and english): it has to be very simple.

At least it should be clearly documented. But I think it is better to be solved.

I have implemented the write portion only (I still have ack=false, therefore no reading) using your async I/O code. The situation didn't improve. I added logging to the code to report any occurrences when the buffer is only partially written on the stream. No hits so I'm still investigating.  
BTW, in your write method, if the os.write call only wrote part of the buffer but not all, local variable 'left' will contain the remainder that wasn't written. However upon return we get 'not not left' which is True if there was a remainder and False if the entire buffer is written. But the information about how much of the data was written, and the data itself is lost. I would think that we want to retry writing just the remainder, and not the entire buffer which would cause the portion that was initially written by os.write to be repeated.

This is actually from the 2.0.1 git tag.

I think the reason it works in your C++ application is in ... the Makefile.
Basically you are linking directly to an alternative GSSAPI implementation, instead of linking to the standard GSSAPI and loading the Globus mechanism using the standard mechglue interfaces to do that.

The confusion arise from the fact that unfortunately in GSSAPI the GSS API and the plugins SPI use the same symbol names so it is something unclear whether you are using the system GSSAPI or directly linking to a mechianism.

However the differences are evident at execution time. Specifically if you link directly to the mechanism code an OID is not necessary because there is no mechanism, to select as you are calling directly into the mechanism by linking into it. When you use an actual GSSAPI library with a mechglue instead you have to use an OID to identify the mechanis.
Now that OID does not have to be specified by the mechanism code, it is instead specified in the mechglue configuration that loads the mechanis shared object.

For example this is how the NTLMSS GSSAPI mechanism condifugration file for GSSAPI looks like:
https://github.com/simo5/gss-ntlmssp/blob/master/examples/mech.ntlmssp.in

Searching for the Globus GSI OID ends up finding this code:
https://github.com/jglobus/JGlobus/blob/master/gss/src/main/java/org/globus/gsi/gssapi/GSSConstants.java which declares 1.3.6.1.4.1.3536.1.1 as the GSI OID (3536 is the Globus identifier as per IANA registry and seem the same OID is found also in some openssh patches to enable use of the GSI mechanism).

So I would guess what youneed is to load the globus GSI shared object in GSSAPI by using a /etc/gss/mech.d/gsi.conf  file with gsi as the name, the OID above as oid and the patch to the globus .so file.

Once you do that, you will be able to sue the GSI mechanism (to the exclusion of any other mechanism) by specifying the above OID in your code, alternatively if you want to support multiple mechanisms you can still pass the NULL OID, this will initialize credentials for all the mechanism that recognize the name and have matching creds and defer to the context establishment code to select an appropriate mechanism.

HTH.

Uuuuh, other @scikit-image/core devs can chime in, but I'm pretty sure this requires a deprecation path. This is required whenever the output of a function will change for the exact same input. When people update their environment, they should not be left to wonder which of the updates changed their results (NumPy, SciPy, pandas, scikit-image could all be culprits in a complex pipeline).

IMO this was at best undocumented behavior, at worst a bug. I lean toward bug. Removing dimensions at will isn't something that should be taken lightly.

As a bridge for anyone who came to depend on this "feature", we could 

* Raise an informative error if the input is 4D *and* includes a singleton dimension. Tell the user that we don't assume this anymore; a volumes with a "dummy channel" are no longer supported. Yes, it breaks, but they get told how to fix it.
* Warn any users with 3D inputs that include a singleton dimension that their input was assumed to be a 3D volume, not a 2D image with a "dummy channel". If they wanted the 2D behavior, *they* should apply `np.squeeze` or slice that channel out of a multichannel array.

The burden should be on the user to provide the right input.

If we allow these failures, I am pretty sure no one will ever look at them.

Perhaps we should only do the --pre installs on our nightly builds, and have those emails go out to a wider audience?

Hi Louis, yes, the behavior you're seeing is an artifact of the change added to address this issue with how new tables appeared in LibreOffice Writer. Now, when a table is created, the columns widths are each set to a proportional fraction of the page width, but also the width of each *cell* in each column is set to match the column width.

What we haven't gotten done yet is to carry this logic through to cell widths on assignment to Column.width. The reason being the logic is not trivial in the general case where one or more cells can be merged. In those cases, the cell width should be the sum of the column width of all the columns it spans.

I've added a new issue for it here #161.

In the meantime, your table creation code can be cleaner with a column width setting function that does the needful, something like this:
```python
def set_column_width(column, width):
    for cell in column:
        cell.width = width

table = document.add_table(rows=10,cols=2,style="ColorfulList-Accent5")
set_column_width(table.columns[0], Cm(13))
```

If you have merged cells, you'll need to do the needful to ensure those cells get the appropriate different width.

By the way, your width setting can be a little simpler using the Cm() constructor:
```python
from docx.shared import Cm

column.width = Cm(13)
```

> A higher level of complexity is the semantics/processes of it and when to use which signature for what.

I touched above on the decision strategy "which signature for what".
It seems simple enough to me.  Presuming people instinctively know the
difference between testing what the branch promises (i.e. did it fix
the add-to-basket bug on Opera?) vs reviewing the branch code
(i.e. reading, understanding, and suggesting improvements to every
line of code), the decision tree seems rather simple to me:

1. Have you only tested the feature?  Use "tester" signature.
2. Have you also reviewed the code in detail?  Use "reviewer" signature.

Do you have an example whether the decision tree would be more complex than that?

> If you only have one signature, you only have one thing to learn and one thing to remember.

... but you also express only one level of engagement with it.  Which
is why I'm probing whether this is enough.

> Probably I lack direct experience on this, but does this distinction
> matter in the end, as wouldn't developer D anyway look at the
> current issue and fix it at hand regardless if it was lightly
> reviewed or not before?

In theory, D could look at the code and only the code, and find the
bug that way.  In practice, there is a practical difference in getting
to fix the problem faster, if you know who did what parts and who
approved it.  (Kind of social engineering meets computer engineering.)

If people study both the code and the authors and the reviewers, they
can better estimate which parts were well reviewed, and which parts
were not.  This may help speeding up the bug fixing.

The role of multi-level signatures here is the following.  OT1H, if it
is enough to know that Joe did this thing and Jane "approved" it, we
can simplify signatures to `Signed-off-by` only.  OTOH, if it is
useful to know that Jane only tested, but not reviewed, the code, then
we may prefer to stick to the current decision tree that we have in
place now.

The above real-life numbers show that we have 259 testing signatures
vs 651 reviewed signatures.  The difference does not look negligible,
hence I'm probing into respective pros/cons...

> For my own clarification, I guess it is increased barrier for the
> reviewer/integrator as the developer will anyway add signatures as
> directed by reviewer R to update the PR.

Yes.  The original developer only uses `Signed-off-by` signature when
he/she prepares the branch.  The reviewer will then let the original
developer know whether to add `Acked-by`, `Tested-by`, or
`Reviewed-by` seal of approval before the branch gets merged.  This
means roughly three things: (1) newcomers use `Signed-off-by` only
anyway, so the entrance barrier is not really there; (2) it is the
reviewer who decides upon his level of involvement, from light (just a
tester) to heavy (full reviewer); (3) proposing to use `Signed-off-by`
for reviewers also would raise the entrance barrier to get the feature
branch integrated *indirectly*, because people would have to look for
reviewers, not only for testers or "ackers", before the branch may be
accepted.  This is what I had in mind.

Looking at your issue, are you imagining that every DOMWidget would have a .save() method that would instruct the frontend to take a screenshot using html2canvas and send it back to python? We'd have to deal with (or educate the user about) the limitation that the saving would have to be asynchronous - we couldn't just save and then use the file in the next line. Also there would be the confusion of trying to run the code with no frontend, or in a frontend that doesn't support widgets, or in multiple frontends (which one should do the rendering?).

Perhaps it's more natural to have a 'save to png' option (button, or whatever) in the frontend that would download a png of the map from the browser? That's the approach bqplot took. That way the widget can control exactly what is imaged and how it is imaged.

Note that the `get_state` method takes several arguments that may help here. You can give it a key or list of keys to filter the state by:

```python
from ipywidgets import *
x = IntSlider()
x.get_state(key=['value', 'min', 'max', 'step'])
```
and you can also have it drop any key that is equal to the default:
```python
from ipywidgets import *
x = IntSlider(value=10)
x.get_state(drop_defaults=True)
```

Now we can try to resolve conflicts using:

`SHELL_PLUS_MODEL_ALIASES`:
Pros:
+ For single model developer can set any alias, which he want.

Cons:
- Developer need to find all conflicts manually and define aliases in configuration.
- Developer need to remember to add alias in settings, each time when creating model with conflicting name.
- This dictionary can be large if there are lot of collisions.

`SHELL_PLUS_APP_PREFIXES`:
Pros:
+ If prefixes for all apps are defined, new conflicts are resolved automatically.

Cons:
- Developer need to remember to add alias in settings, each time when creating new app or each time when first conflict in existing app occurced.
- When prefix is specified, ALL models from app are prefixed, and import without alias is disabled for them. 
So when i have 10 models in app and one of them has the conflicting name, than i need to access 9 other models using prefix.
- In most cases we need to add many similar items to this dictionary like: {<app_name>: <app_name>}
- User can define only prefix and _ separator is forced.

No solution is perfect so ability to add user defined function is needed. 
In my opinion it should has lowest priority and work only if:
- `SHELL_PLUS_MODEL_ALIASES` has no `<app_name>` key or `SHELL_PLUS_MODEL_ALIASES[<app_name>]` has no key <model_name>
- and `SHELL_PLUS_APP_PREFIXES` has no key `<app_name>`

If I recall correctly, the difference this makes becomes apparent when you look at the case where you want to use some credentials from a specific file to talk to a service.

Old way:

```python
from gcloud.credentials import get_for_service_account_json
from gcloud import pubsub

credentials = get_for_service_account_json('/path/to/key.json')
credentials = credentials.create_scoped(pubsub.SCOPE)  # <-- The thing I don't want to have to type...
connection = pubsub.Connection(credentials=credentials)
pubsub.set_default_connection(connection)
pubsub.Topic('topic_name').create()
```

(Also note that in `gcloud.datastore` `SCOPE` was inside `_implicit_environ` which made the code much less readable....)

New way

```python
from gcloud.credentials import get_for_service_account_json
from gcloud import pubsub

credentials = get_for_service_account_json('/path/to/key.json')
connection = pubsub.Connection(credentials=credentials)  # Scoping happens here, where we know we need it...
pubsub.set_default_connection(connection)
pubsub.Topic('topic_name').create()
```

Is there a reason why you'd want to keep the "scope credentials" action separate from the "create connection" action? I can't think of a reason but maybe you guys know more about this... If there isn't then I fully support this change -- seems much more convenient to do this when creating the connection (and expecting the connection to know which scopes are necessary on the credentials you supplied).

```python
client = client.from_service_account_json("service_account.json")

```python
client = bigquery.Client.from_service_account_json("service_account.json", project=project_id)
```
